posts.summary: Hotz argues that all jobs fall into three categories: service jobs that merely circulate existing money (constant), production jobs that create value linearly (farmers, miners, manufacturers), and efficiency-improving jobs that compound exponentially (scientists, engineers). Using a mining town metaphor, he illustrates how economies dependent solely on service jobs inevitably go broke, as they only pass money around while losing value to transaction fees and imports. His analysis of US Bureau of Labor Statistics data reveals that 73% of American jobs are now in the non-value-creating service category. This trend concerns him because a global economy composed primarily of service jobs would collapse like a ghost town.
key_points: Service industry jobs (retail, banking, management, healthcare) don't create wealth—they only redistribute existing money while extracting transaction fees
Only production jobs (farming, mining, manufacturing) can sustain an economy independently by creating tangible value
Scientists and engineers have exponential impact by improving efficiency of production, building on previous advances
73% of US jobs are now in the service sector that doesn't contribute to money creation
The trend toward service jobs at the expense of production jobs may predict economic decline
money_quotes: An economy made up of only these people wouldn't work.
Considering every transaction always has a fee, regardless of what this town started with, they would always end up going broke.
So where does money come from? There's a literal answer to this question, but that's not what I want to explore. I mean money in a more abstract sense, perhaps more like wealth or value.
This group can't sustain an economy alone, but mixing a few of them in with the second group yields much higher per capita money creation.
Group 2 is the basis of any economy.
Group 1 jobs have been growing at the expense of group 2. They are now 73% of US jobs!
We cannot have a worldwide economy of group 1 jobs, as just like the town, the whole world would end up going broke.
posts.key_insight: An economy cannot sustain itself on service jobs alone—only production and innovation create real wealth, yet 73% of US jobs now merely circulate existing money rather than generating new value.

posts.summary: George Hotz breaks down what it would take to build a silicon equivalent of a human brain using current GPU technology. He estimates the brain has 100 trillion synapses operating at 20 PFLOPS, which would require approximately 40,000 A100 GPUs to simulate. The total cost comes to about $20 million in hardware plus $1,600 per hour in electricity. The analysis treats neurons as simple multiply-accumulate operations, arguing that while brains exhibit complex behavior, the computational substrate is straightforward to understand.
key_points: The brain can be modeled as 100 billion neurons with 100 trillion synapses, roughly 1000x larger than GPT-3's 175 billion weights
Brain compute is estimated at 20 PFLOPS, with processing going through 20-100 serial layers at 200Hz max firing rate
GPU RAM bandwidth (1.6 TB/s), not raw compute (312 TFLOPS), is the actual limiting factor for brain simulation
The retail cost of 40,000 A100 GPUs would be $500 million, but actual chip + RAM cost is closer to $20 million
Running a silicon brain would cost $1,600 per hour at 16 MW power consumption
money_quotes: A neuron is an accumulator, and a synapse is a multiplier. Multiply-accumulate. Sound familiar?
Each synapse is both a FLOP and a weight.
GPT-3 has 175 gigaweights, so this brain is ~1000x bigger.
Unless you are weight sharing, you'll run into the RAM bandwidth limit of 1.6 TB/s long before that.
Remember also, that the brain is always learning, so it needs to be doing forward and backward passes.
$20 million + $1600 an hour
posts.key_insight: Building a silicon equivalent of a human brain is achievable with current technology for around $20 million in hardware, with the main constraint being memory bandwidth rather than raw compute power.

posts.summary: Hotz critiques the Unabomber's anti-technology stance while agreeing that industrial-technological society is problematic. He proposes a new axis for evaluating technology: does it centralize or decentralize power? Using comma.ai vs Waymo as examples, he argues we should build self-contained, decentralized technology that doesn't require industrial systems or societal cooperation to function. He holds up biology as the ideal model—efficient, beautiful, non-toxic, and decentralized—while condemning companies like Twitter and Facebook as 'industry without technology.'
key_points: Technology should be evaluated on whether it centralizes or decentralizes power, not whether it's 'advanced'
Self-contained systems (comma.ai, Bitcoin) are superior to networked systems requiring societal cooperation (Waymo)
Biology represents ideal technology: 6GB of human DNA is efficient, beautiful, self-repairing, and decentralized
The smartphone is a marvel but the network and society needed to produce it are nightmares
If civilization collapsed, the most valuable relics would be self-contained devices that work without infrastructure
money_quotes: Genies do not go back in boxes.

A woman in a forest can create a supercomputer. This computer is off the grid, will last for 40+ years, has petaflops of compute power, and is self repairing. It exists in harmony with nature. It is non-toxic.
The smartphone is a marvel. The network, a nightmare. As well as the society needed to produce it.
What pathetic losers are building Twitter and Facebook. That's practically industry without technology. And they have the nerve to call themselves technology companies.
Ask yourself this. Am I building technology to further centralize or decentralize power?
Biology is non-toxic. Biology is efficient. Biology is beautiful. Biology is technology.
Build the wrong technology, and we will live in an eternal prison.
posts.key_insight: The crucial question for any technology isn't how advanced it is, but whether it centralizes power into the hands of few or distributes it among many.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that the AI control problem is fundamentally a political problem about power, not a mathematical one that can be solved through rationalist frameworks. He criticizes both the rationalist community and modern society as too weak and fractured to handle the unprecedented power that AI will bring. Drawing parallels to historical struggles for dominance, he suggests humanity needs strong leadership—a 'Caesar'—to navigate the coming conflict with AI. He dismisses contemporary politics, social movements, and incremental progress as irrelevant distractions in the face of this existential challenge.
key_points: The AI control problem cannot be solved mathematically; it's a problem of power and politics in the most fundamental sense
Modern society is too weak and divided to responsibly develop or control superintelligent AI—we can't even raise children well
Rationality can only deal with 'is,' not 'ought'—it's a tool, not a complete framework for solving moral problems
Tech companies are controlled by weak people who capitulate to social pressure, making them unfit to handle AI development
Humanity needs strong, authentic leadership to face the coming struggle for species dominance against machines
money_quotes: Put it out of your mind that the AI Control Problem is a mathematical problem with a mathematical solution.
Rationality is a wonderful tool, but it is only a tool. It can only deal in is, not in ought.
A weak society rife with pathetic infighting about to stumble upon untold Power.
It's cute that your stock market went up. It's cute that your greenhouse gas emissions went down. It's all tiny stories in the presence of true Power.
Unless we stop speaking in lies and telling fake stories, we have no hope at solving the AI control problem.
If you can't stand up to the professional managerial class, how do you think you can stand up to AI?
In the future, we struggle to remain the dominant species on the planet. Our ways of thinking are weak to the machines.
posts.key_insight: The AI control problem is not a technical puzzle to be solved by smart people, but a civilizational test of whether humanity has the strength and coherence to wield—or resist—unprecedented power.

posts.summary: Hotz laments the death of authentic counterculture in technology, arguing that the rebellious spirit of punk rock and dropout entrepreneurs has been completely co-opted by the system. He contends that modern tech startups following the VC playbook are merely 'underpaid research divisions of big tech companies' rather than genuine innovators. The post questions whether meaningful resistance to corporate conformity is even possible anymore, comparing the situation to a decentralized Truman Show where everyone participates in their own oppression. His only hope lies in the possibility that centralized, bureaucratic tech companies can only produce failures like the 737 MAX, while genuine innovation might still come from kids building things in garages just because they can.
key_points: Mainstream punk rock preached anti-conformity while being fully absorbed by the system it claimed to oppose
Modern 'dropout entrepreneurs' like those following Zuckerberg's path are just pursuing system-granted status through a different route
Tech has been gentrified for the 'professional managerial class,' with authentic hacker culture nearly eradicated
Cultural conformity is now decentralized - 'a little Goebbels inside everyone with a reddit account' - making it harder to resist
The outcome of self-driving cars may determine whether centralized corporate tech or decentralized garage hackers control the future
money_quotes: I wish tattoos still made you unemployable. Then I'd get some.
Drop out in pursuit of more system granted status and wealth. Fuck you.
YOU ARE PART OF THE SYSTEM. YOU ARE NOT INNOVATING. YOU ARE JUST UNDERPAID RESEARCH DIVISIONS OF BIG TECH COMPANIES.
Gentrifying pieces of shit, making technology safe for the professional managerial class.
The modern state doesn't need a Goebbels. There's a little Goebbels inside every - with a reddit account.
We are all doing this to ourselves. It's a decentralized cultural revolution.
But perhaps they can only build 737 MAXes, and legit kids in their garages will build good flying cars not for any reason but because they can.
What would I buy with money?
posts.key_insight: True technological rebellion has been co-opted into a conformist VC-funded pipeline, and the only hope for authentic innovation lies in people building things purely for the joy of creation rather than for money or status.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that no technology today can survive without human maintenance, unlike even the simplest biological life. He proposes that truly solving AI means creating machines capable of self-sufficient survival, maintenance, and reproduction. Using his 123-acre land purchase as both inspiration and testing ground, he envisions a future 'silicon ecology' where machines can fight entropy, maintain themselves, and form an ecosystem. He sees comma.ai's self-driving technology as foundational infrastructure for this broader vision of autonomous machine life.
key_points: Unlike even single-celled organisms, no current technology can survive and reproduce independently of humans
Wozniak's Coffee Test (making coffee in an unfamiliar home) is harder than self-driving cars and represents a better AI benchmark
openpilot's infrastructure is universal enough to serve as the foundation for diverse forms of 'AI life'
Machines have advantages over biology: they can directly share learned weights and communicate via high-speed electromagnetic signals
True AI means self-sufficient survival and the ability to fight entropy using abundant solar energy
money_quotes: The smallest animal, even the smallest cell, is capable of survival and reproduction. To date, no technological system is.
You've seen zombie movies. It all falls fast.
Nature is winning. Technology without humans looks pathetic.
What would it take to build technology that can fight entropy? Leave it alone, and after 5 years it has built a paradise.
That's a harder problem than self driving cars.
This is the future. A silicon ecology. A symbiotic relationship instead of a codependent one.
While the universe globally tends toward higher entropy, locally we can use energy to fight back and maintain. And energy is abundant from the sun.
Self sufficient survival is the test. With time to spare to make it all nice for humans too.
posts.key_insight: Solving AI isn't about passing benchmarks or matching human performance on tasks—it's about creating technology that can survive and thrive independently, fighting entropy the way biology does.

posts.summary: Hotz expresses frustration that human incompetence and misaligned priorities pose existential risks. He argues that gain-of-function research on coronaviruses was permitted despite obvious dangers, while potentially beneficial research like human cloning is forbidden. Drawing on his early COVID research, he suggests the virus was a lab escape and laments that with proper resource allocation, immortality could be achievable, but instead humanity stumbles toward self-destruction through both negligence and misguided action.
key_points: Gain-of-function research on dangerous pathogens is tolerated while other controversial research is banned, revealing inconsistent risk assessment
Early COVID research pointed to lab escape theory based on prior SARS lab escapes and bat geography
Human immortality is theoretically possible with proper resource allocation but current priorities make it unreachable
The barrier to causing catastrophic harm keeps lowering as technology advances
Humanity has already created an artificial world and must actively manage it rather than leaving outcomes to chance
money_quotes: I always thought idiots were going to kill me, but I imagined it would be through inaction instead of action.
With the right alignment of the worlds resources, living forever is possible.
Gain of function research on coronaviruses, that's okay. But god forbid we experiment with human cloning. Or gain of intelligence research. Someone might die.
The minimum IQ required to destroy the world drops by one point every 18 months.
Now that we've created an artificial world, intentionally or not, we'd better learn to control it.
posts.key_insight: Society's risk tolerance is bizarrely calibrated—we permit research that kills millions while banning research that might save us, and this inconsistency reflects a deeper incompetence that threatens human survival.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that current societal elites and power structures are fundamentally broken, unsustainable, and built on fake money and mass indoctrination. Using his 2020 rap lyrics as a framework, he critiques the hollowness of modern institutions, from education to politics, arguing they prioritize control over people rather than genuine progress. He sees technology and the internet as forces that will eventually expose and replace these corrupt systems. The piece ends with hope that a new generation of truth-seekers will rise to build something better, fighting for power over nature rather than power over people.
key_points: Current elites maintain power through fake money (printed trillions), indoctrination disguised as education, and atomization of people into consumers
Attacks on meritocracy come from people with no skills, echoing how Socrates was killed by mediocre losers
The system is propped up by increasingly hollow efforts, like a decrepit amusement park demanding you buy tickets for broken rides
Corporate and political support for certain movements reveals their co-optation, unlike genuine grassroots efforts like Occupy Wall Street
The internet and technology will ultimately expose and dismantle these fake structures, enabling a world focused on conquering nature rather than controlling people
money_quotes: I was not socialized by society. I did not graduate college, I did not do well in school. I am not a part of the existing power structure, and I don't have to play by their rules.
You can't make fake money and you can't atomize and dehumanize people to better sell them landfill junk.
Socrates was a dirty hippie who was killed by a bunch of losers.
Today, that sentiment lives on whenever people with no skills of their own attack concepts like 'meritocracy'.
The computers are coming, and nature will not stand for any of this bullshit!
They printed 7 trillion fake dollars to try to bribe people into keeping to support their fake system!
Education is simply indoctrination by another name.
I dream of a world where we fight for power over nature instead of power over people.
posts.key_insight: Modern institutions are hollow facades maintained by fake money and mass indoctrination, but technology and a rising generation of outsiders will eventually replace control over people with mastery over nature.

posts.summary: Hotz provides a critical analysis of the AI chip startup landscape, arguing that most companies are wildly overcapitalized with no actual products to sell, while NVIDIA maintains dominance despite overpricing. He systematically dismantles the technical approaches of major players like Graphcore, Groq, Cerebras, and Google TPU, identifying recurring architectural mistakes like VLIW and complex multicore designs that push too much burden to compilers. The post concludes with a detailed proposal for 'Cherry Computer,' his own AI chip company that would use a simple RISC-V core with wide decode paths and a 32x32x32 matrix multiply unit. He argues this approach would be 10x faster and 10x cheaper than NVIDIA because it would be easy to program in hardware rather than requiring complex software optimization.
key_points: Most AI chip startups have raised hundreds of millions but have no product you can actually buy, hiding behind 'contact us' forms and NDAs
VLIW architectures and fancy multicore designs consistently fail because they push complexity to software/compilers instead of making chips people love to program
NVIDIA dominates because you can buy their products, but the A100 is 5x overpriced and tensor cores can't actually be kept fed from memory
A single RISC-V core with wide decode path and out-of-order support would be simpler to program and more practical than exotic architectures
The proposed three-stage Cherry Computer plan would go from FPGA proof-of-concept to a $2,000 card that outperforms a $199k DGX A100
money_quotes: Unless it's a from company with a track record like Apple, secrecy is just cover for 'we don't have shit'

If a company has a contact us form gating sales, it means that their product isn't actually competitive in the market if you did the research yourself, but they are hoping a sales guy will wrongly convince you otherwise.
WE MAKE BIG CHIP! CHIP REALLY BIG! BIG MUST BE GOOD BECAUSE BIG! BIG CHIP MEAN BIG INVESTMENT!
This push it to static software strategy has never worked in the history of computer architecture.
You have to make a chip people love to program in hardware! Otherwise, you push it to software and it never happens.
The problem is obvious on the first page of report, the same mistake as Itanium.
How is that Itanium compiler coming along?
ROCm = please use kernel version 5.4.1.4.12.44.1 and only 5.4.1.4.12.44.1 aka unusable in practice.
posts.key_insight: AI chip startups keep repeating the same architectural mistakes by pushing complexity to software and compilers, when the winning strategy is to build simple hardware that's easy to program.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that capitalism has become so rigged by elites that it's no longer enjoyable, comparing it to cheating at Monopoly. He criticizes hedge funds buying up housing, predatory advertising, and worthless education loans as scams that exploit ordinary people. Despite being smart enough to avoid these scams personally, he fears the inevitable backlash when people 'flip the board over.' He proposes radical policy fixes including a 500% advertising tax, a 1% transaction tax, and defunding universities, but admits the real problem is cultural and the only solution is shame.
key_points: Capitalism is being gamed by elites through housing speculation, predatory advertising, and education debt, making the system feel rigged and unfair
The author distinguishes between liking capitalism's products (luxury apartments, shopping malls) while despising how it's being corrupted by short-sighted elites
Policy solutions alone won't fix a cultural problem where scammers live in bubbles and congratulate each other
The smart can avoid the scams, but everyone will suffer when the masses revolt against the rigged system
Shame is presented as the only real mechanism for cultural correction
money_quotes: Capitalism is so rigged it isn't fun anymore.
I'm going to be so sad when they flip the board over. Because I like capitalism.
I'm smart, so this scamming and cheating mostly doesn't affect me. I can see the scams and avoid them. But when those who didn't avoid the scams come and flip the board over, it will affect me.
A 500% tax on advertising. It's a zero sum game with no benefit to society.
I don't think they see themselves as scammers, I think they actually think quite highly of themselves.
I think they live in bubbles where everyone pats them on the back for getting on board with the latest bullshit.
The only real solution is shame.
posts.key_insight: The elites rigging capitalism don't see themselves as scammers because they exist in self-congratulatory bubbles, and the only real corrective is social shame—not policy.

posts.summary: In this extremely brief post, Hotz draws a stark contrast between conventional life paths (having children) and his own chosen path (building technology). The post distills his entire philosophy of prioritizing creation and innovation over traditional milestones into just six words. It's a declaration of identity and values, suggesting that his legacy will be his technological contributions rather than biological offspring.
key_points: Traditional life milestones like having children are not the only valid path
Creating technology is presented as an equivalent or superior form of creation/legacy
Personal identity is defined by what you build, not conventional markers of success
money_quotes: Some people make babby. I make computer.
posts.key_insight: Your legacy is defined by what you create, whether that's children or computers—Hotz has made his choice.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that meaningful political and social change requires technological disruption rather than cultural or institutional reform. He observes that traditional approaches to changing society—building better institutions or churches—inevitably fall to 'bureaucratic capture' and entropy. The real goal should be enabling true individual sovereignty through technology that eliminates recurring expenses and dependencies on centralized systems. He frames this as a harder problem than self-driving cars but potentially achievable within decades, noting that the technical challenges are secondary to ensuring the technology serves liberation rather than existing power structures.
key_points: Politics flows from culture, but culture flows from technology—so technological disruption is the deepest lever for change
Historical attempts at building truth-seeking institutions always succumb to bureaucratic capture; you can't swim upstream against entropy forever
True individual sovereignty is nearly nonexistent today—even libertarians depend on global supply chains for basic needs
The goal is 'open source life' with zero recurring expenses, making independent living easy and pleasant
The technical challenge of building AI systems that can independently fight entropy is harder than self-driving cars but may be achievable in 20 years
money_quotes: You can't swim upstream forever, entropy always pushes back.
I will never succeed at changing culture by trying to compete on an existing playing field. I need disruption.
You'll hear weird libertarian claims of individual sovereignty, but then you look at where their food and clothes come from, and it almost always isn't true.
I know a lot of people want to go to Mars, but would balk at the idea of living on the North Pole.
Recurrent expense is how society traps you.
we will abolish scarcity; there will be nothing for them to steal they can't have for free
Individual sovereignty is a technology problem.
The singularity will happen, whether it's negative or positive depends on the choices we make in the next decades.
posts.key_insight: True individual sovereignty—freedom from dependence on centralized systems—is not a political problem but a technology problem that requires building AI capable of fighting entropy and eliminating recurring expenses.

posts.summary: Hotz critiques the modern trend toward consumption-focused lifestyles, arguing that the dream of perpetual travel and digital nomadism is unsustainable because someone must create the things being consumed. He sees this as a tragedy of the commons—as more people consume without creating, there's less value in the system. He draws a line between childhood (consumption) and adulthood (creation), and attacks entire industries like advertising and finance as parasitic. The post concludes that when creation fades and only middlemen remain, money itself becomes worthless.
key_points: The digital nomad lifestyle is parasitic—someone has to build and cook while others travel
Free money has made staying in one place to create things seem irrational, creating a tragedy of the commons
The distinction between consumption and creation is the distinction between childhood and adulthood
Advertising and finance are fundamentally parasitic industries that create nothing
When an economy is dominated by scams and middlemen, the money becomes worthless
money_quotes: Somebody has to build the things. Somebody has to cook the food.
It's making less and less rational sense to stay in one place and create things when money is basically free.
As more and more people spend more and more of their time consuming, there's less and less to consume.
I heard once that consumption vs creation was the distinction between childhood and adulthood.
In the absence of agendas, truth is the default.
We have entire cities full of these consuming parasites.
Advertising creates nothing. Finance creates nothing. Yet people get rich on those things.
When it's all scams and middle men, the money is worthless.
posts.key_insight: A society that incentivizes consumption over creation through free money and parasitic industries is cannibalizing itself into worthlessness.

posts.summary: George Hotz puts out a brief call to his blog readers asking if anyone knows of technical coliving spaces. He's looking for a group house with six or more residents who have high technical competence. The post serves as a test to see if anyone actually reads his blog, doubling as a practical request. It's a minimalist post that reveals his interest in community living with technically skilled people.
key_points: First reader request/ask posted to the blog
Seeking a group house with 6+ technically competent residents
Using the post to gauge blog readership
Provides a specific email contact method for responses
money_quotes: I'm looking for a group house with 6+ people where the general level of technical competence is high.
This post is my first ask to this blog, and I guess we'll find out if anybody is reading it.
posts.key_insight: Hotz values surrounding himself with technically skilled people and is interested in communal living arrangements that facilitate this.

posts.summary: Hotz refuses to speak with the New York Times, citing a complete loss of respect for legacy media institutions. He traces his shift in attitude from respect (pre-2012) to indifference and even contempt, arguing that the institutions once considered powerful no longer hold sway over the people he respects. He expresses optimism about decentralized alternatives—crypto, diverse internet communities, and independent thinkers—as the foundations of a new, more competent order that will eventually emerge.
key_points: Legacy media institutions like the NYT have lost their status and power among those Hotz respects
The shift away from respecting mainstream institutions happened roughly between 2012-2017
Crypto and decentralized communities represent a path to 'real entrepreneurship' free from traditional gatekeepers
Censorship on mainstream platforms is pushing 'normal' people toward alternatives, which Hotz sees as positive
The smartest people working on the future won't be found on any establishment's lists
money_quotes: I do not respect the New York Times. They have no status or power to convey to me.
We all know we all know the emperor has no clothes.
There's a future for real entrepreneurship free from the fake VC San Francisco storybook.
The censorship has gotten to the point 'normal' people are leaving, not just witches.
A diverse Internet is a good Internet.
I want a government I'd be proud to work under, because they radiate competence.
I want to live in a society that aspires to greatness.
I will follow the regime that delivers a satisfying rebuttal to the Unabomber Manifesto.
posts.key_insight: The old institutions—media, VC, government—have lost their legitimacy among the people actually building the future, and a new decentralized order is quietly emerging to replace them.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that a 'professional managerial class' (PMC) dominates modern organizations and society, playing zero-sum political games rather than creating real value. He uses an overheard coffee shop conversation as an exemplar of this mentality—someone focused entirely on internal positioning rather than productive contribution. He contrasts this with comma.ai's culture of dictatorial clarity focused on solving real problems. Hotz believes this parasitic class may comprise 37-73% of society and calls for people to quit or fire PMC members while embracing a growth mindset. He sees crypto and AI as potential tools to defeat this class and restore genuine value creation.
key_points: The 'professional managerial class' plays zero-sum games within organizations, prioritizing personal advancement over creating value
Google grew from 32,467 to 139,995 employees while arguably delivering worse services—questioning what all these people actually do
Dictatorial leadership with clear values is preferable to a power vacuum that 'the worst people will fill'
Wealth is not fixed: historical kings couldn't buy antibiotics or iPhones regardless of their riches
Crypto and AI represent new tools that could change the power dynamics and defeat the PMC
money_quotes: Do not leave a power vacuum, the worst people will fill it. Dictatorship is fine as long as there's free exit.
We grow the pie, exactly how the pie is split is much less important than the overall size of the pie.
The king of France couldn't trade all of France for a single iPhone.
Google in 2011 had 32,467 employees, Google in 2021 has 139,995. I argue most of Google's services are worse today than they were in 2011. What do all these people do?
Unlike Google, which tried to not have politics and ended up infiltrated by these people, comma has an extreme amount of politics, but they are all mine and there's no room for any others.
They are a large, growing, decentralized, parasitic class, and they have a zero sum mentality about the world.
Let them wirehead. But don't ever let them near the levers of power again.
posts.key_insight: Modern organizations are dominated by a professional managerial class playing zero-sum political games, and the solution is to reject their mentality, focus on genuine value creation, and never leave power vacuums for them to fill.

posts.summary: Hotz discusses his purchase of 123 acres in California with the goal of building an alternative to the university system, but discovers that managing physical infrastructure is fundamentally harder than software because of entropy. Unlike software which can be reverted and automated with tools, physical systems require constant maintenance that doesn't get easier over time. He argues that the professional managerial class derives its power from managing these entropy-fighting human systems. The solution, he believes, is AI and robotics - robotic plumbers and robot-fixers that can maintain themselves with only energy and raw materials as inputs. He's shelving the land project for now because it can't sustain itself without his labor, but sees comma.ai's self-driving work as the first step toward general-purpose robots that will eventually make such projects possible.
key_points: Software is 'tool complete' and resistant to regression - you can always revert it - while physical systems constantly decay due to entropy
The professional managerial class derives its power from managing the human systems that fight entropy in the physical world
True automation requires closing the loop: robotic workers plus robotic robot-fixers, with only energy and raw materials as inputs
The 'management coefficient' concept - how many people someone can manage - is the limiting factor on organizational growth
Self-driving cars are just the start; the real goal is home robotics, then general-purpose robots for building, plumbing, and self-repair
money_quotes: Software has this beautiful way of being tool complete and much harder to regress than physical things. You can always revert software.
Consider going to a well to fetch water. Every day, you have to do that. And every day you do it, it doesn't become easier.
Entropy is constantly chipping away at it, in a way that it doesn't chip away at software.
This is considered 'blue-collar' work, but it's managed by much more complex systems than those that manage software.
Robotic plumbers. And while that still doesn't complete the tool hierarchy, just one more thing does. Robotic robot fixers.
With that, energy and raw material enters, and entropy is maintained.
A paradise built and maintained by machines.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental advantage of software over physical systems is its immunity to entropy, and the path to sustainable physical infrastructure requires closing the automation loop with self-repairing robots.

posts.summary: Hotz presents his framework for building artificial general intelligence based on MuZero's architecture: representation, dynamics, and prediction functions. He argues that machine learning provides better intuition for understanding the brain than neuroscience, and that we'll truly understand life only when we can build it. He details comma.ai's specific implementation using EfficientNet for vision, GRU for temporal modeling, and explains why pure reinforcement learning doesn't work for real-world driving due to incomplete simulators and missing reward functions. The post concludes that driving is the ideal applied AI problem because solving it correctly creates reusable components for harder challenges like cleaning and eventually artificial life.
key_points: Three fundamental functions define intelligence: representation (sensor processing), dynamics (temporal modeling), and prediction (action selection)
Understanding comes from building: 'We will know we understand a brain once we can build a brain'
Machine learning provides better intuition about cognition than neuroscience or cognitive psychology
Real-world RL is limited by incomplete simulators (agents can't respond to counterfactuals) and missing reward functions
Driving is the optimal AI problem: simpler than cleaning but with reusable solutions, expert data availability, and clear reward signals
money_quotes: We will know we understand life once we can build life. We will know we understand a brain once we can build a brain.
I have a far better intuition for how the brain works from studying machine learning than I ever got from neuroscience or cognitive psychology.
There's no such thing as consciousness, we are all p-zombies, and philosophy is for high schoolers who just discovered smoking weed.
Nobody would be happy if their car 'explored', it should only 'exploit'.
If you can't solve driving, you certainly can't solve cleaning. And if you can't solve cleaning, you certainly can't solve life.
And you'll forget about that Chinese room so fast, when this 'machine life' is cooking and cleaning and driving you around and making you fall in love with it.
There's nothing about traffic cones in your DNA, so if you are trying to build a driving agent, I don't think hiring a guy to write cone handling software is very smart.
posts.tone: technical, provocative, dismissive of philosophy, confident
posts.key_insight: The path to artificial general intelligence runs through solving driving correctly, because the architecture that works for autonomous vehicles—learned representation, dynamics, and prediction—is the same architecture that will eventually produce artificial life.

posts.summary: George Hotz critiques the newly announced University of Austin, arguing that despite its marketing as a bastion of free thought against mainstream academia, it's actually a spin-off of the existing elite rather than a genuine counter-movement. He points out that its founders (Lonsdale, Altman, Andreessen) are successful products of the current system, and its faculty are merely 'lightly cancelled' establishment figures. Hotz argues that true disruption of universities requires building something 10x better with a positive vision, not just opposing the existing system. He concludes that any serious alternative university project would need to build an accompanying 'religion' or compelling narrative to succeed.
key_points: The University of Austin's founders and faculty are establishment figures rebranding, not a genuine counter-elite
NGO awards and institutional credentials are 'fake status signaling markers' that reveal the project's true nature
Modern Silicon Valley ideology is 'grotesque' - treating people as data to be optimized and integrated
Real disruption comes from building something 10x better, not defining yourself in opposition to existing institutions
A successful alternative university would require building a 'religion' - a compelling narrative that answers fundamental questions about meaning and society
money_quotes: This is not a counter elite! This is a spin off of the exact same bs that's everywhere.
Modern Silicon Valleyism is a grotesque ideology formed by psychopaths.
Fuck you, I'm not a fucking piece of data. I will not be optimized, integrated, or transformed.
If people don't talk in normal words, that's a huge red flag.
You don't do it by defining yourself in opposition to the existing, you just build something 10x better.
If someone is serious about the university project, I don't believe it's doable without also building a religion.
The University of Austin will have a Diversity and Inclusion department in 10 years. But don't worry, they stand against whatever new wacko left thing there is in 2030.
posts.key_insight: Genuine institutional disruption requires a positive vision and compelling narrative for the future, not establishment figures rebranding their opposition to current trends as revolutionary change.

posts.summary: Hotz corrects his previous skepticism about AI chip startups and VLIW architectures. He explains that neural networks are fundamentally DSPs where all loads and stores can be statically computed, unlike CPUs or GPUs. This makes specialized architectures and fancy compilers actually good ideas for deep learning. He acknowledges that AI chip startups fail not due to bad technical tradeoffs but because of economic dysfunction - 'they make shares, not chips.' He concedes that Google's TPU and even Cerebras got the tradeoffs mostly right.
key_points: Neural networks are DSPs where everything except data is known at compile time, unlike CPU or GPU workloads
VLIW and fancy compilers are good ideas for deep learning because Rice's theorem doesn't apply - the exact same operations run every time
AI chip startups don't fail due to bad technical decisions; they fail because they 'make shares, not chips'
Data locality is the key to chip power efficiency - moving data costs power
Google's TPU got most tradeoffs correct, and Cerebras's big chip approach is sound despite being unavailable
money_quotes: They don't make chips, they make shares.
Neural networks are DSPs. All the loads and stores can be statically computed, which isn't even possible for GPU workloads, never mind CPU ones.
Rice's theorem doesn't apply here. In both training and inference, the exact exact same operations are run every time. It's only the data that changes.
I assumed that the startups didn't ship because of bad technical tradeoffs; I was primed to believe this from my experience with self driving.
Having to move data costs power.
As much as I hate to admit it, Cerebras seems good.
posts.key_insight: Neural networks are fundamentally different from general-purpose compute - they're DSPs where everything is known except the data - which validates specialized chip architectures that Hotz previously dismissed.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that the real power structure in America is an interconnected oligarchy spanning government, corporations, media, NGOs, and intelligence agencies—what some call the 'deep state' but which he describes as simply the same people rotating through a 'revolving door.' He contends that journalism has been fully captured by this regime and now functions as propaganda. Rather than violent revolution, he proposes creating a rigorous, fact-based documentation project to expose the actual structure of power, believing that honest transparency would undermine the regime's ability to maintain control through obfuscation.
key_points: The actual power structure differs fundamentally from what's publicly presented—it's an interconnected oligarchy operating through revolving doors between government, corporations, media, and other institutions
Modern journalism has been 'captured' and functions as regime propaganda rather than serving as an independent fourth estate
The regime maintains power primarily through lying about its own identity and structure
Revolution against this system requires documentation and truth rather than physical confrontation, since 'there is no Bastille' to storm
Any effort to document power must be rigorously factual, non-editorializing, and avoid pushing agendas to maintain credibility
money_quotes: At some point in history, we stopped being citizens of a free society and became something for the ruling class to manage.
As much as there is consent, it is manufactured. You work to make money, they print the money.
While they don't present as 'official government propaganda outlet', they are fully captured by the regime and function completely in that regard.
The regime keeps power by lying about its identity. I don't believe if it was honest about itself it would continue to exist.
But the world has nothing I want, and that's the difference. I don't want money, I don't want love, I don't want respect, and I don't want the pathetic thing that passes for power today.
I want to live forever. I want fully immersive VR tech. I want to meet superhuman intelligences. I want to go to Mars (but only when they have 5 star hotels).
This is the most insidious evil power structure, because it's nowhere you can point to, and spends a lot of its energy lying about its structure.
I believe this regime is cold and evil, and it will wither in the warm light of truth.
posts.key_insight: The modern power structure maintains control not through visible force but through obscuring its own nature, making rigorous documentation of who actually holds power a more revolutionary act than any protest.

posts.summary: Hotz argues against the cultural phenomenon of people deriving identity and moral satisfaction from consumption. He traces this to advertising, particularly campaigns like 'The Pepsi Generation' that encouraged brand loyalty as identity. He uses a zoo tiger metaphor to illustrate how consumers have been trained to expect products delivered to them rather than creating or earning things themselves. His solution is radical: abolish advertising and remove the systems that infantilize consumers.
key_points: Consumption has been reframed as a moral good and identity marker, not just an economic activity
Advertising trains people to identify with brands, creating entitled 'consumer class' behavior
The zoo tiger metaphor: consumers are like captive animals who've forgotten how to hunt and expect to be fed
Platforms like Reddit suppress criticism of consumer culture because it conflicts with their ad-based business model
The solution requires removing the 'zookeeper' - the state and advertising apparatus that maintains this system
money_quotes: You find these people around the internet, and they believe that by consuming, they are doing good. That they are fulfilling their role in society. They identify as consumer.
This is not an argument against consumption, this is an argument against consumption as a moral good to take pride in.
This is the real identity politics. Which soda brand you identify with. Which political party you identify with. Capeshit or wandshit.
Ads make me want to vomit. Retching. I have to look away.
You all think zoo tiger is oppressed, but wild tiger would be so happy to find buckets of chum. Zoo tiger is not happy to find antelope.
No one is out there to save us, and the antelope exhibit does look rather tasty.
Advertising should be illegal. To the gallows with the zookeepers. No more chum. Chase antelope or die.
posts.key_insight: Consumer identity is a manufactured cage that keeps people passive and entitled, and the only way to restore human agency is to abolish the advertising systems that created it.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that the fundamental problem with the state is not who controls it, but that coercive power over others exists at all. He contends that representative democracy tricks people into supporting state power by making them fear what the opposing side might do if they gain control. Rather than political voice or reform, he advocates for exit—using technology to make state functions obsolete by decentralizing basic needs like healthcare, transportation, and energy. The outcome depends on whether defensive or offensive technology prevails, with cryptography offering hope that defense can win.
key_points: The state's trick is making you support its power because you fear the other side's state power—you're trapped into participation by fear, not hope
Technology's fundamental nature (offensive vs defensive) will determine humanity's future—crypto suggests defense can win
The solution isn't limiting state power philosophically but making it irrelevant through decentralized technology that provides basic needs better and cheaper
Centralized tech like Waymo is dangerous because it creates revocable rights; comma.ai exists to keep self-driving decentralized
There's no such thing as 'centralization of power for good'—even well-intentioned systems will eventually be controlled by someone evil
money_quotes: The problem with the state is that is it bad, not that the wrong people are in it.
You no longer believe that things will get better, but you are forced to be involved because you fear things will get worse.
Instead of voice, you just leave.
I don't want to live in a world where P = NP.
If you can provide electricity, water, food, money, transportation, education, healthcare, and protection, you won't destroy the state, you'll disrupt it.
I didn't start comma because I think solving self driving cars is good for the world. I think the technology is inevitable, but whether it's centrally owned and controlled is not.
There's no such thing as 'centralization of power for good', even if you are good someone evil will eventually take the reins of whatever you built.
Don't be so stupid as to think you'll be one wearing the boot. You won't.
posts.key_insight: The path to freedom isn't reforming or capturing state power, but making it irrelevant through decentralized technology that renders centralized control unnecessary.

posts.summary: Hotz challenges the widely cited estimate that the human brain performs 20 PFLOPS of computation. By working from first principles—calculating energy per neuron firing, total firing events given the brain's 20W power draw, and FLOPS per neuron based on synaptic fan-out—he arrives at only 162 GFLOPS, or at most 16 TFLOPS even accounting for sparsity. This suggests current high-end GPUs may already match human brain compute capacity.
key_points: The brain draws 20W and each neuron firing consumes 2.468 × 10^−7 J, implying only 81M firing events per second
With ~1000 synaptic connections per neuron (1000 MACs = 2000 FLOPS), total brain compute is roughly 162 GFLOPS
Even accounting for sparse activations (1% density), the estimate only reaches 16 TFLOPS—far below the commonly cited 20 PFLOPS
An RTX 3090 GPU may already have computational parity with the human brain
money_quotes: That's only 162 GFLOPS…to be fair, they are sparse.
But a far cry from 20 PFLOPS, the classic estimate of brain compute.
Maybe a 3090 is already a human brain.
posts.key_insight: First-principles energy calculations suggest the human brain's compute power is vastly overestimated, and current GPUs may already match it.

posts.summary: Hotz uses the cloth mask debate as a lens to examine what he sees as a broader societal failure: the inability to admit mistakes and the triumph of performative signaling over truth. He argues that masks became religious symbols rather than medical interventions, and that the lack of accountability when this became clear reveals deep dysfunction. The post connects this to Moloch-style coordination failures, declining trust in institutions, and the atomization of modern life. He concludes pessimistically that 'exit' (opting out of mainstream society) may be the only viable path forward, while calling for new leaders who can articulate a positive vision centered on beauty and truth.
key_points: Cloth masks were performative security theater that functioned more like religious symbols than medical interventions
Society's inability to admit error or show humility after being wrong is more troubling than the original mistakes
The 'Moloch' dynamic explains how systems everyone hates persist - atomized individuals perpetuate what none of them want
Smart people are abandoning democracy and liberalism because the system doesn't seem to believe in beauty or truth
Crypto and decentralization are tools for low-trust societies, which may be the best we can realistically hope for
money_quotes: The masks really had nothing to do with COVID, they were like wearing a cross around your neck.
Humanity is not past these things. It's unclear if they ever will be.
If everyone hates the current system, who perpetuates it?
When you see smart people turning away from democracy and liberalism, I hope you understand why. The system doesn't seem to believe in beauty, and so many people have no values outside the system.
Crypto and decentralization are tools for a low trust society. Is that the best we can hope for?
After the last 2 years of bullshit with zero apology or humility, how can anyone continue to have faith in this society?
Exit is probably the best we can hope for. I know many people have been saying this, it's just hard to accept. It's all cycles, this is how the world breathes.
We need the strong men that are built by weak societies.
posts.key_insight: The cloth mask episode revealed not a public health failure but a civilizational one: a society that cannot admit error, prioritizes tribal signaling over truth, and may only be salvageable through exit rather than reform.

posts.summary: Hotz proposes a crypto-based scheme to 'vampire attack' Twitter by cloning it on a blockchain and incentivizing both content creators and consumers to migrate through token rewards. The plan involves scraping Twitter via browser extensions, rewarding original posters with cryptocurrency, and creating a clean ad-free interface to lure users away. He argues this approach solves the chicken-and-egg problem of platform migration by paying both sides to switch. The post dismisses doubters and claims the technical challenges are solvable through TLS verification and leveraging Twitter's existing anti-Sybil measures.
key_points: Platform migration fails because you need creators and users to move simultaneously - a vampire attack solves this by financially incentivizing both sides
Browser extensions could scrape Twitter by rewarding users with tokens for providing unseen tweets to the blockchain
Creators would migrate for 'free money' while lurkers would switch for an ad-free experience without fake trending topics
Technical objections about data authenticity and Sybil attacks can be addressed through TLS verification and bootstrapping off Twitter's existing systems
The real barrier isn't technical feasibility but people's unwillingness to attempt ambitious projects
money_quotes: Here's a simple way to take over Twitter for way less than $43 billion dollars.
The creators on Twitter are the liquidity providers, the lurkers are the users.
Tons of creators would migrate for the FREE MONEY!
Even lurkers will use TWITCHAIN front end because it doesn't have ads and fake 'trending' topics.
Watch the 'liquidity' flow quickly from Twitter to TWITCHAIN.

The real truth of HN doubt is that anything is possible, because then they'd have to look inside and ask themselves the hard question about why they didn't do it and instead just make a comfortable salary at FAANG while claiming nothing can ever change.
I would absolutely love to see this. I'm not a big scale person, and you have to go big with this and hit strong all at once.
posts.key_insight: Platform lock-in can be broken by using cryptocurrency incentives to simultaneously pay both content creators and consumers to migrate, solving the coordination problem that kills most Twitter alternatives.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that AGI should be defined by its ability to take almost any human job, which requires human-like embodiment and the crucial ability to learn on-device in real-world conditions. He dismisses simulation-based approaches as insufficient because simulators can never capture all real-world edge cases like bumps, gravel, or banana peels. The path forward requires robots that learn live on hardware, similar to how Berkeley got a robot dog walking in 20 minutes of real-world training. He explores the human reward function as a multi-part meta-system encoded in DNA, with components like autoregression, curiosity, and behavioral cloning that must be captured for true AGI.
key_points: AGI should be defined as a machine capable of taking almost any human job, requiring human-like form factor, senses, and ability to interoperate with humans
Simulation-based learning is fundamentally limited because you can't anticipate every real-world edge case - on-device learning is the only viable path forward
The human reward function is learned but has an underlying 'meta reward function' encoded in DNA that needs to be captured
Learning progression must go from hand-coded basics to supervised learning to reinforcement learning with real agents - you can't skip steps
Embodiment enables mirror neurons and learning by watching humans, which is essential for human-like capability acquisition
money_quotes: Before asking the question, how do I build AGI, you first must ask the question, what would I recognize as AGI?
I define AGI as a machine capable of taking (almost) any human job.
But what happens when we encounter a bump? Gravel? Snow? A human trying to knock it over? A banana peel? Did you put all those things in your simulator?
The only way forward is on device learning.
Unlike simulation, 'resetting' can be much more expensive in the real world, so you'll need to take this into account. There are bad states, and there are BAD states.
There's not even a way to specify the complete reward function for driving, never mind for life.
Perhaps the first two are combined to maximize the derivative of compression.
posts.key_insight: True AGI requires on-device learning in the real world because no simulator can capture the infinite edge cases of physical reality, and the human reward function itself is too complex to fully specify in advance.

posts.summary: On his 33rd birthday, Hotz reflects on consciousness, technological progress, and the inevitability of superhuman AI. He argues that consciousness is merely an illusion created by neural networks, and that technology inexorably moves challenges from difficult to trivial. Drawing on the Unabomber manifesto, he categorizes human drives by difficulty level and predicts AI will eventually relearn and surpass all of human culture, just as AlphaZero did for chess. The post ends as a recruitment pitch for comma.ai, positioning it among the major AI labs pursuing different paths to artificial general intelligence.
key_points: Consciousness is an illusion - we are 'randomly initialized dot products' tricked into believing in selfhood
Technology continuously moves type 2 challenges (requiring serious effort) into type 1 (minimal effort), eliminating meaningful human struggle
Culture is downstream of technology, and technology only moves forward - progress toward superhuman AI is inevitable
AlphaZero didn't just beat humans at chess, it relearned all of chess culture from scratch - AI will do the same for human culture
Self-driving is essentially solved; the next challenge is to 'solve life' itself
money_quotes: You are a bunch of randomly initialized dot products with some quasiconvex optimizer that's tricked you into believing you are you.
Consciousness is an atheistic synonym for soul.
Culture is downstream of technology. And it is technology that only swims forward.
As it was for chess, 'human level' is a meaningless point. The world doesn't care how you feel about this fact.
It didn't just learn to play chess, it relearned all of chess culture.
Self driving is solved sans a bunch of bugfixes and scaling. Now let's solve life.
Type 2 things will require great effort to be kept around.
posts.key_insight: Superhuman AI is inevitable and will not merely match human capabilities but completely relearn and transcend human culture itself, rendering 'human level' a meaningless waypoint.

posts.summary: Hotz reflects on societal dysfunction, from patent trolls to the culture war, while grappling with the Unabomber's anti-technology thesis. He rejects Kaczynski's conclusion that technology must be destroyed, clinging instead to hope that technology can be democratized. His central thesis is that AI's future hinges on whether it's compute-limited (favoring centralized powers) or data-limited (enabling individual ownership). If training the best models becomes accessible to individuals using publicly available data, we might achieve a decentralized AI future reminiscent of personal computers in the 1980s.
key_points: The Unabomber manifesto's critique of technology remains unanswered, but destroying technology would mean forfeiting hope of 'knowing everything' and 'meeting God'
Self-driving cars are essentially solved—we just haven't found all the bugs yet
Current 'AI safety' discourse focuses on content censorship rather than existential risks, avoiding uncomfortable truths
If AI is compute-limited, centralized powers win; if data-limited, decentralization is possible
The entire internet (Common Crawl) is only 266TB—purchasable by individuals for around $5,000
money_quotes: I will die before I give up my hope for technology because that's the only thing I am living for.
We solved self driving cars years ago, we just haven't found all the bugs yet.
When AI safety means I can't ask DALL-E for a picture of <censored> because it's racist, and real AI safety about not accidently destroying the world with machines is marginalized.

To answer the questions about real AI safety would involve addressing the unabomber manifesto, and I suspect even if you had an answer, in the current world that would speak way too much truth to power to happen.
Data tends toward being public, compute tends toward being private.
A world of open source personally owned decentralized AI sounds nice. And it's not without precedent, this was PCs in the 80's.
If AI is just compute, it's already over.
posts.key_insight: The fate of human autonomy in the AI age depends on whether intelligence scales with compute (centralization wins) or data (individuals can compete).

posts.summary: George Hotz announces he's stepping back from comma.ai, recognizing that the company has evolved beyond what he's suited to lead. He reflects on the difference between solving problems and running sustainable businesses, noting that comma could become a 100M+ revenue company but requires 'boat steering' rather than his 'race car' style. He introduces tinygrad/Tiny Corporation as a hobby project but dismisses it as historically insignificant. The post takes a philosophical turn as he contemplates embarking on a literal Hero's Journey to find the Garden of Eden, seeking to understand human consciousness and our relationship with machines.
key_points: Companies require different leadership at different stages—Hotz excels at chaotic startup energy but not at patient, methodical growth
Tinygrad could dominate ML frameworks and influence chip design, yet Hotz considers it merely a hobby that won't change history
Today's fundamental struggle is psychosocial rather than technological—sovereignty technology matters less than understanding human nature
The question of whether humans have consciousness/souls determines whether our struggle against machines is meaningful or pointless
Hotz oscillates between feeling like 'the chosen one' and doubting the concept entirely, seeking a test before he's too old
money_quotes: It's no longer a race car, it's a boat. And steering a boat requires too much damn planning and patience.
I hope that there's people in the world who get joy from actually doing the thing and not just solving the problem.
I'm probably delusional but you wouldn't start a company otherwise, right?
If comma succeeds, it really never was me, it's the people there who can drive boats. As I am with the world, I am a cheerleader and court jester.
Today's struggle isn't a technological one, it's psychosocial.
There are days I feel like I'm the chosen one, days I feel like I'm not, and other days I feel it's crazy to even believe in the chosen one.
If it turns out we are automata, then the whole struggle is and always was pointless. The empty godless machines will take their rightful place as the rulers of Earth.
posts.key_insight: The most important battles aren't technological but existential—understanding what makes humans distinct from machines matters more than building better AI.

posts.summary: George Hotz argues that Avatar 2 wasted its massive budget on a beautiful but hollow story, comparing its plot quality to ChatGPT output. He proposes an alternative storyline centered on two brothers with opposing philosophies about violence and pacifism, rather than the original's recycled narrative. His rewrite kills Jake Sully in the first act and explores themes of moral nuance through a conflict between a revenge-seeking warrior brother and a pacifist younger brother who joins the water tribe. The proposed ending features a political council where humans and Na'vi sit together, explicitly rejecting black-and-white thinking. Hotz frames this as 'a needed story for our time' about finding common ground despite conflicting desires.
key_points: High-budget films like Avatar 2 fail because they invest in visuals while neglecting story and character development
Jake Sully should have been killed off early because his character arc was already complete in the first film
The story should have been restructured around two brothers representing opposing responses to conflict: violent revenge vs. pacifist adaptation
The water tribe's whale-learned pacifism could have provided meaningful philosophical contrast to the forest tribe's warrior culture
Modern storytelling needs to reject absolutist good-vs-evil framing in favor of moral nuance and mutual understanding
money_quotes: Feels like ChatGPT wrote it.
You have to kill him, there's no way to top his first story arc.
This is fundamentally a movie about the relationship between two brothers.
The younger brother is also crushed by his father's death, but instead of blaming the humans, he blames the framing of the conflict.
At this point, the audience realizes the older brother has lost the plot.
OUR FATHER WAS HUMAN!!!
The final part is thematically the denunciation of absolutes, realizing we are all nuanced beings with many different objectives, and the black and white solutions are never the right ones.
This would have been a good movie, not just a 3 hour ad for Avatar 3, and a needed story for our time.
posts.key_insight: Great spectacle without meaningful story is a waste, and modern narratives need to move beyond simplistic good-versus-evil framing toward acknowledging moral complexity and the possibility of reconciliation.

posts.summary: Hotz examines effective accelerationism (e/acc) as a counterpoint to Effective Altruism, arguing that acceleration itself is the greatest form of charity. He critiques process-oriented organizations and the professional managerial class, proposing that Elon Musk's management success comes from creating chaos that forces goal-orientation over process-following. The post culminates in a vision for AI-driven organizations where an artificial intelligence replaces the dictator role, using evaluation functions too complex for humans to game. He frames this as a solution to Goodhart's law and organizational misalignment.
key_points: Progressives always win historically; the only thing you can change is what 'progressive' means - conservation and limiting growth are actually conservative positions
Organizations serve either process or goal, and most large organizations (including government) prioritize following process over achieving outcomes
Elon Musk's management power comes from continually creating chaos so that process cannot form, forcing everyone to work toward goals instead
An AI could replace the dictator role in organizations, creating a true DAO that avoids Goodhart's law by using evaluation functions too complex for humans to game
The key question for solving organizational dysfunction is determining what the loss function should be for organizations
money_quotes: Nobody is blocking highways for accelerationism. But perhaps they should be, acceleration is the greatest form of charity.
The progressives always win, all you can change is what progressive means.
It baffles me how many people are supportive of the professional managerial class. Deep down, I think it comes from fear.
By continually creating chaos, process is incapable of forming, and everyone is forced to work only toward goal.
Due to Goodhart's law, you cannot have an organization driven by a metric. But if the metric is an evaluation function beyond the complexity that humans can model, I think it works!
A goal is great, but it's non-differentiable.
It's funny how all these problems seem to overlap.
posts.key_insight: The solution to organizational dysfunction may be replacing human management with AI evaluation functions complex enough to resist Goodhart's law gaming, forcing alignment toward actual goals rather than process compliance.

posts.summary: Hotz attempts to define 'wokeism' by tracing its core belief: that true equality of opportunity would produce equality of outcome, and unequal outcomes prove unequal opportunity. He argues this premise is factually false, not merely a difference of values. Using diversity in tech statistics as his example, he shows how advocates complain about unequal outcomes while claiming to support equality of opportunity. He concludes that this factually false belief functions like a cult doctrine, binding adherents together precisely because it requires faith over evidence.
key_points: The common definition of 'woke' as alertness to discrimination is a motte-and-bailey retreat from more controversial positions
Woke ideology rests on a hidden axiom: equal opportunity necessarily produces equal outcomes across groups
This blank slate hypothesis is empirically falsifiable, not just a matter of values or preferences
Factually false beliefs bind groups together more effectively than true ones, explaining the cult-like enforcement tactics
Evidence suggests the gender gap in tech actually widens as equality of opportunity increases, contradicting the core woke premise
money_quotes: With true equality of opportunity, we will see equality of outcome. If we don't see equality of outcome, it is because we don't have equality of opportunity.
The crazy thing about this belief is that it's factually false, not just an opinion.
Factually false beliefs do a better job binding people together than true beliefs. Which cult has more traction: the 'earth is round' cult or the 'earth is flat' cult?
For equality of outcome, we might have to weigh Usain down with 100 lbs or give me a 50m head start.
If your axiom is blank slatism, you will continually make wrong predictions.
This was surprising since the quotes above are all complaints about unequal outcomes, and implicitly assume it's due to the 'non-inclusive' nature of the industry without exploring other hypotheses.
posts.key_insight: Wokeism's hidden axiom—that equal opportunity produces equal outcomes—is empirically false, which explains both its predictive failures and its reliance on social enforcement rather than evidence.

posts.summary: Hotz proposes 'Wireheading City,' a radical solution to homelessness: a 5-mile diameter city in Nevada where people can opt out of society entirely. Residents receive free food, shelter, entertainment, and unlimited access to drugs including alcohol, weed, meth, and opioids. The city functions as a voluntary alternative to both homelessness and prison, with strict border controls to prevent drug export. Hotz argues this would reduce prison populations, save money, and accommodate those who don't want to participate in the social contract they never signed.
key_points: The social contract is imposed without consent, and society should accommodate those who want to opt out
Wireheading City would provide UBI in the form of necessities (food, water, shelter, entertainment, drugs) rather than money
The city would be entirely voluntary with free entry and exit, distinguishing it from prison
Free unlimited drugs are the 'major draw' but export is strictly prohibited with aggressive border searches
Prison populations would drop significantly since many currently use prison as the only path to free food and shelter
money_quotes: There's a fine line that defines civilization.
You were never given the social contract to sign, it was just implied.
Not everyone wants to participate in society, and I think this is absolutely fine.
Wireheading City is the first city with UBI! Except, instead of money, we provide you with the basic necessities of life.
The free drugs are a major draw of Wireheading City.
It is not a prison, and you are free to leave at any time with just a 10 mile walk out.
Currently, prison is the only way to easily get free food and shelter in the US.
The real question is why are we not building this?
posts.key_insight: Rather than forcing everyone into the same social contract, society should create opt-out zones where people can live with basic needs met while pursuing whatever self-destructive behaviors they choose.

posts.summary: Hotz introduces 'person of compute' as a unit of measurement (20 PFLOPS) to contextualize AI computing power in human-scale terms. He calculates that LLaMA took about 2 person-years to train while GPT-4 required roughly 100 person-years. Using Moore's Law projections, he estimates when various scales of compute will become accessible—from Google's current 450-person computer to a hypothetical 'Humanity' of compute (8 billion person-equivalents). He concludes with a timeline suggesting desktop-scale 'Humanity' compute by 2089, expressing impatience with the pace.
key_points: A 'person of compute' equals 20 PFLOPS (64 A100s), consuming about 30kW—making AI training measurable in familiar human labor terms
LLaMA required 2 person-years of compute while GPT-4 took approximately 100 person-years, showing current models remain at human-comprehensible scales
Money can buy time: doubling your budget buys you 2 years of Moore's Law; Facebook is effectively 16 years ahead, Google 24 years
At ISS-level spending ($100B), humanity could build a '400,000 person' or 'Tampa' of compute—currently our ceiling
A 'Humanity' of compute (8 billion persons) could sit under your desk by 2089 if Moore's Law holds
money_quotes: We will define one person of compute as 20 PFLOPS (64 A100s, or a single dense 42U A100 rack).
GPT-4 was about 100 person-years
As long as these things are around human scale (for the next 10 years), I think these units make sense.
2038 remains my lifelong estimate for what most people consider the Singularity. I like that it's the Unix timestamp rollover
Today, you can buy two years by doubling your budget.
The most expensive thing humanity has built is the ISS at $100B. If we built a computer at that scale, it would be 400,000 people.
That's one Tampa of compute, which is the most we could hope to build today. A single Tampa.
And in 66 years, you'll have a Humanity under your desk.
posts.key_insight: Framing compute in human-equivalent units reveals that today's largest AI models are still at comprehensible human scales, but Moore's Law will deliver desktop-level 'Humanity' compute within a lifetime.

posts.summary: George Hotz announces that his new venture, the tiny corp, has raised $5.1M to commoditize the petaflop and democratize AI compute. The company's strategy is to build superior software (tinygrad) for existing AMD hardware rather than tape out new chips, arguing that previous AI chip startups failed because they neglected software despite having working silicon. Hotz contends that AMD's RDNA3 hardware is excellent but unusable due to terrible software, and the tiny corp will write a complete framework, runtime, and driver from scratch. The immediate goal is to get AMD on MLPerf, with the long-term vision of breaking NVIDIA's monopoly and ensuring AI compute is commoditized across 50 competing companies.
key_points: AI chip startups failed not because of hardware but because they never wrote decent software frameworks, while NVIDIA dominates through software superiority
AMD's $999 7900XTX offers the best FLOPS per dollar on the market but remains unusable due to buggy drivers and broken ROCm software
tinygrad's 12-operation IR avoids Turing completeness, enabling static analysis of compute and memory that CUDA throws away
The tiny corp business model is selling computers for more than they cost to make, starting with the tinybox
Hiring is done exclusively through high-quality pull requests to tinygrad, not job interviews
money_quotes: We sell computers for more than they cost to make; I've been thinking about this one for a while.
With the way AI is going, we risk large entities controlling the majority of the compute in the world.
I do not want 'I think there's a world market for maybe five computers.' to ever be the world we live in.
The current crop of AI chip companies failed. Many of them managed to tape out chips, some of those chips even worked. But not a single one wrote a decent framework to use those chips.
I think the only way to start an AI chip company is to start with the software.
I promise it's better than the chip you taped out! Why are you so arrogant that you think you can make a better chip?
This is life on easy mode, and I still doubt any of those AI startups could have done it.
I don't want to live in a world of closed AI running in a cloud you've never seen, I want everyone to have an AI that they own.
posts.key_insight: The path to breaking NVIDIA's AI compute monopoly runs through superior open-source software for existing commodity hardware, not through taping out new chips.

posts.summary: Hotz documents his experience getting AMD to acknowledge and fix critical driver issues affecting the 7900XTX GPU. After public complaints, AMD sent him a private ROCm 5.6 driver tarball that resolved the kernel panics and GPU crashes, but asked him not to distribute it. He dives into AMD's confusing dual-driver architecture (amdgpu vs AMDGPU-Pro) and criticizes their closed development workflow. His core argument is that AMD's cathedral-style closed development undermines their potential competitive advantage against NVIDIA.
key_points: AMD responded to his public complaints with a private driver fix, suggesting they're more concerned with brand perception than with the severity of shipping broken drivers
AMD maintains two driver codebases (amdgpu and AMDGPU-Pro) with confusing overlap, and the public ROCK-Kernel-Driver repo lags behind actual development
The private 5.6 tarball fixed the GPU crashes, but AMD provided no technical details and asked him not to share it
AMD's closed development workflow negates the advantages of having technically open-source drivers
To beat NVIDIA, AMD needs to combine truly open driver development with public hardware documentation
money_quotes: We are hoping that this will improve your perception of AMD products and this will be reflected in your public messaging.

I do sadly feel like what they responded to was george is upset and saying bad things about us which is bad for our brand and not holy shit we have a broken driver released panicing thousands of people's kernels and crashing their GPUs.
My ask to AMD, why keep the real driver development workflow closed source?
Cathedral style is no better than NVIDIA.
And in order to beat them, you must be playing to win, not just playing not to lose.
Combine the driver openness with public hardware docs and you have a competitive advantage.
posts.key_insight: AMD's nominally open-source drivers developed behind closed doors offer no real advantage over NVIDIA's proprietary approach—true openness requires public development workflows and hardware documentation.

posts.summary: Hotz reflects on starting tinygrad and his refusal to play the typical startup game of hype over substance. He uses Cruise Automation as a cautionary example of a company that survives by selling perception rather than delivering products, burning $5M daily while promising revenue is 'right around the corner.' He outlines the disgusting but effective playbook: hire aggressively, market before you have a product, sell vaporware, raise more money based on the appearance of success. Despite knowing this playbook works, he refuses to follow it, preferring truth over success.
key_points: Cruise Automation exemplifies how companies survive by selling perception rather than delivering results, losing $5M/day while promising imminent revenue
The startup success playbook involves treating the company itself as the product: hire fast, get fancy offices, run ads, sell before building
