Communication in business is often about achieving desired outcomes rather than conveying truth
Value destruction (consuming resources) may matter more than capital efficiency as a measure of company impact
Hotz would rather fail than compromise his integrity by playing the hype game
money_quotes: The point of communication is not to convey truth, it's to acheive desired outcomes.
I know what it takes to succeed, but I'm not willing to do it.
Don't have a product? It doesn't matter! The company is your product.
Nobody is driving in a Zoox, Zoox's aren't real. But that doesn't matter, use photoshop. Perception is reality.
It's only other people's time you are wasting, it doesn't cost you much. You can build a product later.
If you lie to the world you lie to yourself and one day you can no longer tell what's true or not.
I would live under a bridge and eat out of dumpsters if I got to know the truth.
I would rather lose than sell out.
posts.key_insight: The startup world rewards perception over reality, and while Hotz understands this playbook perfectly, he considers following it a form of self-destruction worse than failure.

posts.summary: Hotz predicts desktop computers will match human capabilities in 15 years, mobile phones in 25, and argues this should inspire optimism rather than fear. He dismisses concerns about AI existential risk as misguided collectivism, comparing centralized 'singularity' narratives to communism. The Earth is small and limiting, but space and the metaverse offer infinite expansion. Humanity's future will be radically diverse—people can choose struggle, games, or wireheading—and AI is simply another tool for self-actualization.
key_points: Human-level desktop computing is ~15 years away, mobile ~25 years—presented as near-certainties
The Earth's smallness is why we've dominated it; space and metaverse offer escape from zero-sum thinking
Collectivist narratives (UBI, effective altruism, 'common destiny') are dismissed as communist scams
The real threat isn't AI but cultural singularity—homogenization that reduces human diversity
The future is radically choose-your-own-adventure: struggle, play, or wireheading are all valid paths
money_quotes: In about 15 years, a desktop computer will be able to do everything a human can. In 25, a mobile phone.
The only reason we killed all the animals is because the Earth is small.
Humanity does not have a common destiny!
Good thing everyone knows communists are losers and poor.
Moore's law isn't over, Intel is. Energy usage growth isn't over, USG is.
The scariest plots are the ones that are reducing that diversity, the 'singularity' in culture instead of economic growth.
You are free from your programming. The universe is huge and it's ours.
And like all our tools, AI is only here to help you.
posts.key_insight: The real existential risk isn't superintelligent AI but cultural homogenization—humanity's salvation lies in radical diversity of choice across an expanding universe.

posts.summary: Hotz calculates what it would take to build a computer capable of training GPT-4 in a single day instead of 90 days. Starting from the observation that GPT-4's training used roughly 47.5 person-years of compute, he works through the economics of building such a machine using wafer-scale compute and solar power rather than traditional GPUs and grid electricity. He arrives at a surprisingly feasible $400M budget and frames this as tinygrad's future fundraising goal. The post concludes with a call to find the ideal location for this 'new Los Alamos' of AI compute.
key_points: GPT-4's 3e25 FLOPs training requirement is 'very human scale' at roughly 47.5 person-years of equivalent compute
Wafer-scale compute at 3nm offers 35x better cost efficiency than buying AMD GPUs ($0.20/TFLOP vs $7/TFLOP)
Building your own solar plant at $0.20/watt beats grid electricity and pays for itself in a year at 50% utilization
The complete system could be built for $400M - dramatically less than the naive $5B GPU approach
Tinygrad plans to raise $400M at a $2B valuation to build this, and will sell wafer computers at DGX prices with 10x the compute
money_quotes: If a person has 20 PFLOPS of compute, GPT-4 used 47.5 person-years to train. Very human scale.
I want to build a computer capable of training GPT-4 in a day.
Also, my wafers will be circles, not lame squares.
For power, I'm not getting scammed by the stupid grid.
It's $7 for a TFLOP on AMD. It's $0.20 for a TFLOP on my wafers.
The tiny corp's next raise will be $400M at a $2B valuation. No joke.
Welcome to the new Los Alamos. To the zettaflop and beyond!
posts.key_insight: By vertically integrating wafer-scale compute and solar power, you can build a GPT-4-in-a-day training cluster for under $400M instead of billions - making frontier AI training accessible outside hyperscalers.

posts.summary: George Hotz argues against the AI 'hard takeoff' or FOOM scenario by distinguishing between closed games like Go and the open complexity of the real universe. While AI achieved superhuman performance in games through self-play, the universe contains computers and other players that must be modeled, making dominance impossible without majority compute. He uses the hedge fund analogy to show that predicting markets requires modeling all other market participants, including their computers. Hotz concludes that compute will naturally distribute in a power law, and the real risk comes from artificial restrictions that could create a 'powder keg' where one actor breaks the rules.
key_points: The universe has incomparably more complexity than any game - it's a difference in kind, not degree, since the universe contains the players themselves
AI dominance in games doesn't translate to reality because games don't include the computers playing them, but the real world must model all competing AI systems
The hedge fund analogy: everyone is already trying to predict markets with AI, but you'd need more compute than the entire market to dominate
Compute will naturally distribute in a power law, preventing any single system from achieving dominance
The dangerous scenario isn't unrestricted AI development - it's artificial FLOPS caps that create powder kegs where a single rule-breaker gains asymmetric advantage
money_quotes: AI looked scary. It looked like it was one FOOM away from self playing and becoming superhuman at the universe.
Compression is prediction is intelligence, intelligence is prediction is compression.
Your dynamics model of the universe must include computers. You'll have to spend a lot of effort modeling them.
The computers are active players, and they have a lot of compute. They might even be using it to try to model you!
The smart regulation isn't capping the FLOPS in training runs. That's creating a powder keg.
If you don't want FOOM, you just need to prevent a 51% attack on compute.
More intelligence leads to more new tricks, but the tricks get harder and harder to find.
The information revolution will do for intelligence what the industrial revolution did for energy.
posts.key_insight: AI can't 'take off' and dominate the universe because unlike closed games, the real world contains other computers and players that must be modeled, ensuring compute advantages remain distributed rather than concentrated.

posts.summary: Hotz argues against Eliezer Yudkowsky's AI doom predictions, contending that superintelligent AIs will remain inscrutable weight matrices rather than formal systems capable of 'solving' game theory problems like the prisoner's dilemma. He believes the rise of AI will be slow and predictable, similar to the industrial revolution's muscle machines. The brain operates near physical efficiency limits, and current silicon still has significant ground to cover before matching human computational capacity. Rather than fearing rapid takeoff, Hotz predicts a manageable economic acceleration—doubling every 2 years instead of daily—which he views as exciting rather than catastrophic.
key_points: The core disagreement with Yudkowsky is whether AIs will be formal systems or inscrutable weight matrices—Hotz believes the latter, permanently
Superintelligent AIs cannot 'solve' the one-shot prisoner's dilemma to achieve cooperate-cooperate; practical agents can't prove such properties about each other
Writing zero bugs means coding too slowly; in competitive environments, even superhuman coders will make mistakes
Machines currently have 2 zettaflops versus humanity's 160,000 zettaflops—16 doublings remain before parity
The brain operates near the Landauer limit due to evolutionary optimization for efficiency, and silicon is 10-10000x off this limit
money_quotes: If you are writing 0 bugs, you are coding too slowly, and in a competitive enviroment you will lose.
Search and optimization are not magic, they are just hard.
If I'm negotiating with someone, the last thing I want to do is give them access to my source code!
The rise of the thinking machines will be slow and predictable, just like the rise of the muscle machines was.
As we accept ASI won't be able to crack AES keys, we should also accept that many other kinds of search and optimization are still very hard for them, like one-shotting diamond nanobots!
Doubling every day would be scary, but every 2 years…that doesn't sound like doom, that sounds awesome!
The machines are reproducing about 10x faster.
posts.key_insight: Superintelligent AI will be bounded by the same hard computational limits that constrain all systems, making its rise gradual and manageable rather than sudden and catastrophic.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that modern democracies are unsustainable because many people prefer being managed over accepting the responsibility that comes with freedom. He proposes a three-class society: Citizens who participate economically and have full political rights, Subjects who receive government support but forfeit voting rights and must live separately, and the Incarcerated. This system would allow people to opt into dependency while preventing them from voting themselves benefits that would bankrupt the state. He believes this is the only way to prevent democratic collapse into dictatorship.
key_points: Many people actively prefer being managed over having freedom with responsibility, as evidenced by the Canadian trucker protests and Egyptian fundamentalists
Net positive contribution to society is primarily an economic calculation - if you're not in debt, creating jobs, and paying more in taxes than you receive, you're contributing
Immigration policy should be simple: accept net economic positives, reject net negatives
Democracy inevitably collapses when citizens discover they can vote themselves benefits from the treasury
The solution is voluntary opt-out from citizenship: receive support but lose political influence and live separately
money_quotes: A free society requires personal responsiblity. People here want to be managed. They want a ruling class.
If you don't do crime, have a job, and aren't taking more from the government than you give, you are a net positive.
Anyone who doesn't agree to this basic framework is an idiot who wants to live in a poor country.
A free society isn't for everyone, but the key aspect is that you should be able to choose to opt in or out, but you can't do both.
If you want a life provided to you by the government, I'm okay with paying for this. However, if you choose this life, I don't want to see you, I don't want you in my city, I don't want you voting.
I don't want you influencing society any more than a foreigner.
Do we want to embrace policies like this, or do we want our society to collapse to the point it no longer can?
posts.key_insight: Democracy can only survive if those who choose government dependency voluntarily surrender their political voice, preventing the inevitable vote-yourself-rich spiral that destroys free societies.

posts.summary: The post argues that the most effective way to reduce government power is not through armed revolution but by building decentralized alternatives that outcompete government services. Hotz contends that the US government has been failing since around 1971, pointing to stagnant energy use, ballooning healthcare costs, and ineffective education spending as evidence. He proposes specific disruption strategies: crypto-based payment systems to replace credit cards, private solar grids, AI-powered security systems, and outcome-focused alternatives to education and healthcare. The ultimate goal is creating a society with minimal government reliance, such that any eventual dictator would be weak and ineffectual.
key_points: Government services can be outcompeted by decentralized alternatives that are 10x better, despite decentralization's inherent inefficiency
The US government has been failing since 1971, masked only by America's strong starting position - similar to Google's decline after 2010
Armed revolution is counterproductive; it actually prolongs government power and leads to dictatorship
Crypto payment systems must prioritize convenience over ideology - people care about ease of use first, feeling good second
Public surveillance databases should be available to everyone, not just governments and corporations
money_quotes: You disrupt the government by providing 10x better versions of all of the services that it provides.
Decentralized stuff is inefficient and harder to build. However, if the people running the centralized stuff are too bad of scammers, the decentralized stuff can outcompete it.
Government policy has resulted in a $129 trillion dollar wealth transfer from the young to the old.
When an organization can no longer deliver growth, it has failed.
People with bullshit jobs spend their full time justifying and keeping those jobs, the government has done the same.
Armed revolution is unlikely to get you what you want. Counterintuitively, trying it will prolong the government.
Privacy in public is stupid. There's only so many criminals, why aren't we tracking them all in a public database.
Monarchy is good, but even better: Can you build a high trust decentralized society?
posts.key_insight: The path to reducing government power isn't revolution but building superior decentralized alternatives that make government services obsolete through competition.

posts.summary: Hotz recounts comma.ai's seven-year journey to solve behavioral cloning for self-driving cars. The naive approach of predicting steering angles from images fails because small errors accumulate over time, causing the car to drift. Their initial fix used lane detection as a correction factor, but this broke the end-to-end paradigm—a compromise they call comma's 'original sin.' The core insight is that prediction errors must be anti-correlated (not just uncorrelated) to prevent unbounded drift. After years of progress removing hand-coded components, they believe they've found a solution to end-to-end behavioral cloning, though Hotz leaves the specifics as an exercise to the reader.
key_points: Simple imitation learning (predict action from state) fails in practice because errors accumulate over time—the model never sees the corrupted states it creates
The train/test distribution mismatch is fundamental: during training, images are ground truth; during deployment, each image reflects accumulated errors from previous predictions
Uncorrelated errors aren't enough—you need anti-correlated errors that cancel out, making the sum converge to zero
Lane detection served as a correction function but required hand-labeled ground truth, breaking the end-to-end learning paradigm
After seven years, comma believes they've solved end-to-end ground truthing of the correction function
money_quotes: This turns out not to work, it couldn't even drive straight on highways.
We referred to lanes as the 'original sin' of comma, and tried really hard to remove them.
This model is easy to train, and can achieve very low losses on a holdout set. However, it won't drive a car.
Are the ε correlated? In the best case they aren't, but in practice they almost always are.
You need them to be anti-correlated. You need the limit of that sum to be 0.
The billion dollar question, how do you end-to-end ground truth f_correction?
posts.key_insight: Behavioral cloning fails not because models can't predict actions accurately, but because even small errors compound when predictions alter future inputs—solving this requires anti-correlated corrections, not just accurate predictions.

posts.summary: Hotz responds to Curtis Yarvin's liberal/conservative heroin/cocaine analogy, embracing the 'heroin' side as a self-identified progressive who believes in technological inevitability. He argues that technology only moves forward, that trying to reverse progress would require god-like power, and that humanity's eventual death is thermodynamically guaranteed anyway. He dismisses AI doomer concerns as overblown thought experiments while warning that 'don't build AI' is a dumb strategy more likely to cause civilizational regression. The post concludes with pragmatic nihilism: build the future not because it matters, but because you can.
key_points: Conservatives inevitably lose because time and technology only move forward - reversing progress would require technology more powerful than the current state
Humanity will die eventually due to entropy; this is basic physics, not speculation
Paperclipping AI scenarios are unrealistic because the competitive search processes that create AI won't stop at any single outcome
Overly cautious moves like 'don't build AI' are more dangerous than building, risking civilizational dark ages
The correct stance is pragmatic building without indefinite optimism - create because you can, not because it ultimately matters
money_quotes: Liberals sell heroin, conservatives sell cocaine.
I am not a conservative. Conservatives lose. If you want to not lose, you have to stop being a conservative.
The best thing you can do to make SF better is leave.
Technology is a one-way street. Ironically, it would take immensely powerful technology to try to drive in reverse on that street.
Humanity is going to die. Is this news to you? Did you pay attention in high school physics?
A paperclipping AI makes no sense beyond a thought experiment. The search processes that created that AI will not stop at paperclipping!
Don't build AI is a really dumb strategy.
Build the world you want to see tomorrow. Not because it matters, but because we can. And what the hell else are you doing?
posts.key_insight: Technology's forward march is inevitable and trying to stop it is both futile and more dangerous than accelerating it, so build not out of optimism but because there's nothing better to do.

posts.summary: This piece presents a fictional movie screenplay set five years in the future, following four disaffected college students who plot to sink the world's largest cruise ship as a form of nihilistic terrorism-as-content-creation. The story parallels their planning with an oblivious upper-middle-class family booking passage on the same ship. The plot explores how algorithmic society has stripped meaning from life, leaving youth with nothing but the desire for attention and notoriety. The twist ending reveals omnipresent surveillance thwarted the attack before it began, suggesting the same systems that created their alienation also prevent their rebellion.
key_points: Modern life under algorithmic control has eliminated authentic experience and 'owned space' where people can exist outside prediction and manipulation
The characters view mass violence as the only remaining path to significance in a world where all other forms of meaning have been commodified
The screenplay satirizes both the nihilistic youth (seeking martyrdom for views) and the comfortable professional class (speaking in 'GPT predictive word salad')
Surveillance capitalism creates the conditions for radicalization while simultaneously preventing its expression through omnipresent monitoring
The piece is deliberately provocative, using the format of a movie pitch to explore ideas that would be flagged by AI safety systems
money_quotes: As machines got better and better at predicting behavior, there was less and less owned space. No place where a kid can be a kid.
Everything feels like a Xerox of a Xerox.
We dance for the advertisers. We are the crafted consumer. We exist to consume.
It's no longer conversation, it's some GPT predictive word salad. Helpful, not harmful. RLHFed to shit.
Creation is inevitable, destruction is a choice.
The unabomber without a cause. The trenchcoat mafia without trenchcoats.
This is how you beat the game.
He wonders if that mattered.
posts.key_insight: The same technological systems that alienate young people from authentic existence also surveill them completely, creating a closed loop where meaningful rebellion becomes impossible.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that Ethereum's original vision of being 'the world's computer' was brilliant but has been abandoned with the shift to Proof of Stake. He identifies databases as the key infrastructure that gives big tech its power, noting that standing one up remains frustratingly difficult in 2024. He calculates that a true world computer would need to handle roughly 10 million transactions per second—a million times more than Ethereum's current 13 TPS. He suggests that once a single machine can handle all global transactions, big tech's dominance will collapse.
key_points: Ethereum lost its way by moving to Proof of Stake instead of focusing on building a practical world computer
Database infrastructure is the real moat protecting big tech companies
The world fundamentally only needs one database—multiple instances exist for throughput and sandboxing, not necessity
Global transaction volume is approximately 10 million TPS when aggregating credit cards, social media, email, and other services
Ethereum's 13 TPS is off by a factor of one million from what's actually needed
money_quotes: In 2024, it's still painfully annoying to stand up a database.
This is the only thing big tech really has, to the point that I often use Google Sheets or a GitHub repo effectively as a database.
Another fact about databases is that the world only needs one of them.
One properly built database can kill big tech.
Ethereum is off by a factor of a million.
Once a single machine can handle the worlds transactions, big tech falls.
Who's building the 10M TPS ethereum?
posts.key_insight: Big tech's real competitive advantage is simply making databases easy to use, and a sufficiently capable decentralized world computer handling 10 million TPS would eliminate that moat entirely.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that while Rich Sutton's Bitter Lesson correctly criticizes hand-coding domain knowledge (like 'cone detection' in self-driving cars), it shouldn't be taken to the extreme of evolving AI from scratch. Since our goal is building superhuman AI, not recreating the origin of life, we should reverse-engineer the human brain's architecture rather than letting evolution rediscover it. The brain's components—neocortex, hippocampus, basal ganglia, amygdala, thalamus—are 'hacks' we can replicate with current technology like transformers and TD-learning.
key_points: The Bitter Lesson is correct that hand-coded domain knowledge (cone detection, traffic rules) is ridiculous—humans learn these from data, so AI should too
But taking the lesson to its extreme (evolving AI from scratch like evolution did) is unnecessary since we have a working reference implementation: the human brain
Building AI is like building a radio—reverse-engineering an existing one is faster than recreating the conditions that led to its invention
Human brain architecture (neocortex, hippocampus, etc.) should guide AI architecture, mapping to transformers, RAG, TD-learning, etc.
The goal is 'knock-off humans' that can then solve harder problems—we don't need to solve life itself
money_quotes: Humanity only has one engineering project, building better engineers than humans. After that, the thing we built can do the engineering.
Obviously there's no reference to traffic cones inside human DNA, they learn about them from data. So there shouldn't be any reference to traffic cones in your codebase.
The brain isn't some hyperelegant machine that captures the essence of learning, it's a bunch of hacks, and hacks that we can replicate.
I say the bitter lesson stops at human DNA.
We are just trying to build knock-off humans, not solve life. They can do that.
Having a cone guy in your self driving car company is still ridiculous, because the only working implementation of a driving agent doesn't have a cone guy, it learns cones from data.
posts.key_insight: The Bitter Lesson's rejection of hand-coded knowledge should stop at human brain architecture—we should reverse-engineer the brain's modular structure rather than trying to evolve intelligence from scratch.

posts.summary: George Hotz argues that online dating apps are fundamentally broken due to misaligned business models and poor user experience design. He proposes three major fixes: charge men more to balance gender ratios (citing Seeking's 4:1 female-to-male ratio as proof), de-emphasize pictures in favor of compatibility signals and linked social media accounts, and redesign the apps to function like CRM software with lead qualification metrics. He suggests these changes could be implemented as a third-party overlay on existing platforms, scraping their data to create a better experience.
key_points: Dating apps should charge men significantly more than women to fix the gender ratio problem - Tinder is 75% male while Seeking's $109/month male fee creates a 4:1 female ratio
Pictures poorly predict date quality; compatibility questions and linked social media (Instagram for looks, Twitter for personality) would be more honest and effective
Dating should be treated like sales with CRM-style interfaces: find leads, qualify them, track meet-to-conversation ratios publicly
The Nash equilibrium of radical transparency means nobody gains an edge from hiding information, so everyone should just accept honesty
A third-party CRM overlay scraping existing dating apps could disrupt the industry without platform approval
money_quotes: First off, you need a business model that isn't mobile gaming.
Charge men money per month. Only men. Every club promoter understands this.

With almost no exceptions, they are fairly similar looks wise. The bottom date the bottom, the middle date the middle, and the most attractive date the most attractive. You will end up dating your looks match.
The dating cycle is the same thing as the sales cycle. Find leads, connect, qualify leads, present, overcome objections, close the deal, and nurture.
If you aren't agreeing on a time to meet in the first ten messages, this is a dead lead.
The Nash equilibrium is that nobody will be able to get an edge on anyone else by hiding things, and the sooner everyone accepts that the sooner we can all have a nice experience.
Everyone would have a better time. Except the shit online dating companies, which need to be disrupted.
posts.key_insight: Online dating apps are broken because they optimize for engagement rather than matches; fixing them requires charging men more, emphasizing compatibility over photos, and treating dating like a CRM sales pipeline with transparent success metrics.

posts.summary: George Hotz humorously complains about Google's Knowledge Panel incorrectly listing his height as 5'4" instead of 5'10". He frames this trivial error as serious 'misinformation' and threatens a 'libel suit,' satirically invoking Google's own stated concerns about misinformation during COVID. The post ends with a mock demand for an apology from Sundar Pichai or a settlement of $3.50 via Google Pay.
key_points: Google's Knowledge Panel displays incorrect biographical information about Hotz
The post satirizes corporate rhetoric around 'dangerous misinformation' by applying it to a trivial personal grievance
Hotz mocks the asymmetry between how seriously platforms claim to take misinformation versus their actual accuracy on basic facts
The absurd $3.50 settlement demand underscores the comedic intent
money_quotes: Google, please take this misinformation seriously before you end up in a very large libel suit.
This is not on a site you are linking to, this is first party misinformation from Google.
I know from COVID that you take misinformation very seriously.
I'm expecting a full retraction and a handwritten apology note from Sundar.
I will settle for a retraction and being Google Pay'd $3.50, but only if you handle this issue promptly.
posts.key_insight: If platforms are going to position themselves as arbiters of truth, they should at least get basic facts right about the people they're cataloging.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that America faces three possible decline scenarios: European-style economic stagnation, Russian-style oligarchic capture, or South African-style collapse driven by social resentment. He observes that America's tech dominance depends entirely on attracting skilled immigrants to a merit-based society, but rising crime, difficult immigration policies, and government captured by corporate interests threaten this advantage. After spending three months in Hong Kong, he's seriously considering relocating tinygrad there, finding the society more functional and the government more competent. He sees a potential path forward only if Trump wins and Elon Musk influences policy toward reduced government spending and easier skilled immigration.
key_points: America's tech industry dominance comes from immigration and meritocracy, not inherent American talent—and software production is far easier to relocate than manufacturing
The US government has become 'a cow to milk for money by large corporations and friends of the state' through bills like the CHIPS Act and Inflation Reduction Act
Hong Kong offers a functional society with safety, helpful people, and a competent pro-growth government—political freedom matters less than property rights and physical safety
California's crime problem has reached a breaking point that makes it feel like 'not a society'
The two metrics that will determine America's trajectory: whether government spending decreases from $6.2T by 2028, and whether skilled immigration becomes easier
money_quotes: If America is not in decline, I want to believe America is not in decline. If America is in decline, I want to believe America is in decline.
The combination of unskilled immigration with expensive social welfare is suicide for your country.
The state is mostly just a cow to milk for money by large corporations and friends of the state.
Our society wasn't brought down by the tyranny in 1984 or the hedonism in Brave New World, but instead by the resentment in Atlas Shrugged.
It's a whole lot easier to move software production than steel production.
This society is functional. The people in stores are helpful, the public transport is clean and fast, and not once have I felt unsafe walking alone on the street at night.
While there are some downsides with less political freedom, ask yourself how much that matters to you? Say compared to feeling safe on the street.
I have absolutely had it with the crime, it isn't a society and it's so sad to see.
posts.key_insight: America's tech dominance is entirely dependent on attracting global talent to a merit-based society, and if quality of life deteriorates while immigration stays difficult, that talent—and the industry—will simply move elsewhere.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that America has become a low-trust society with no path to restoration regardless of election outcomes, contrasting it with China where he perceives genuine civic pride and government delivery of tangible results. He dismisses American defenses of 'freedom' as hollow when basic security isn't provided, citing anarcho-tyranny where criminals operate freely while citizens face consequences for self-defense. He concludes that fixing American society requires either generational change or civil war, neither of which he wants to witness. His solution is to bet on the Internet as the next great empire and build for that future while physically locating in high-trust places like Hong Kong.
key_points: Trust in government correlates with tangible delivery: China's growth in electricity and steel production is harder to fake than GDP, and citizens respond to real improvements in their lives
American freedom rhetoric rings hollow when basic security isn't provided - anarcho-tyranny means police ignore bike thieves but would arrest you for confronting them
Neither political side in America offers a path to restored trust; both respond to the other with dismissiveness or hostility that deepens division
The synthesis to America's problems may be obvious to outsiders, but societies in 'mental health episodes' can't be told what's wrong - they must discover it painfully themselves
The future belongs to the Internet as an empire succeeding both American and Chinese dominance
money_quotes: A government delivering growth and better lives to people is one worth trusting. A government that kills squirrels that get too famous on social media, not as much.
When Americans are asked about this, they will defend some nebulous concept of freedom, and I ask, freedom to what?
Of course, had I confronted the guy, the police would have been quick to come for me. Anarcho-tyranny.
When a society loses trust, it takes a lot for it to come back.
The answer may be nothing short of generational change or civil war.
It's like the country is going through a mental health episode and you can't just tell people what's wrong with them in the first therapy session.
The American empire is over, the Chinese empire is next, and the Internet is the empire after that. We build for that world.
posts.key_insight: When a society loses the ability to deliver tangible improvements to citizens' lives and can't even maintain basic order, trust collapses irreparably, and the only rational response is to build for whatever comes next.

posts.summary: George Hotz dismisses typical AI doom scenarios as Hollywood nonsense, instead presenting a more grounded concern: hyper-personalized AI influence agents. He traces the economics of advertising—noting Google earns $428 per American annually—and extrapolates to a future where AI agents study your behavior for years to craft perfectly targeted manipulation campaigns. The endgame isn't rogue superintelligence; it's teams of tireless, aligned AI agents whose sole purpose is altering your behavior, and the only reason this isn't happening now is that you're not yet worth the compute cost.
key_points: Traditional AI doom scenarios misunderstand computers; AI development is more like animal breeding than alien invasion
Advertising already extracts hundreds of dollars per person annually, and the market for influencing behavior is expanding
Political campaigns spend billions but still only scratch the surface of what targeted influence could achieve
AI agents running at 1000x real-time, with years of behavioral data, represent the endgame of targeted advertising
The only current barrier to personalized influence agents is cost—once compute is cheap enough, everyone becomes a target
money_quotes: Most AI doom scenarios are nonsense. The idea of a rogue computer behaving in a way no humans want never made sense to me, only to Hollywood who doesn't know all that much about computers.
Power doesn't stop at the voter, it just flows through the voter.
The marketplace for your behavior is ever increasing in scope.
Imagine a room of 10 CIA agents running at 1000x real time (they have 16 hours for each minute of yours). And their only job is to influence you.
They know more about you than you know about yourself.
They will make sophisticated ad campaigns in real time only to ever be consumed by you. The endgame of targeted advertising.
The only reason you don't have 10 agents doing this right now is that you aren't worth it.
A team of long running extremely competent people with the sole goal of manipulating you. You don't stand a chance.
posts.key_insight: The real AI threat isn't rogue superintelligence—it's perfectly aligned influence agents that know you better than you know yourself, and the only thing stopping them is that you're not yet worth the compute.

posts.summary: Hotz reflects on Trump's 2024 victory and whether Republican control of all branches of government represents real power or merely the appearance of it. Drawing from his experience watching Elon Musk's Twitter takeover, he questions whether the federal government can be similarly restructured. He speculates about whether a 'deep state' actually controls power, and argues that the only way to prove genuine control would be cutting the government budget by over 50%. The post is ultimately about the nature of institutional power and whether it can be dismantled by those who nominally hold authority.
key_points: America goes through revolutionary transitions roughly every 70 years (Washington, Lincoln, FDR)
Watching Elon's Twitter transition changed Hotz's understanding of how power works - having people try to build things and seeing what breaks beats reverse engineering
The key test of real power is the ability to shut something down; Elon could shut down Twitter, but can any government shut down itself?
Hotz acknowledges his own conservative approach to risk compared to Elon's edge-of-leverage style has limited his success
The true measure of whether Trump has real power would be cutting the government budget by 50%+
money_quotes: If you can't shut it down, you don't control it.
Does government power lie there, like we are told, or is it all secretly elsewhere? This is likely the best chance we'll get to find out.
I'm not very good at being a follower, I need to deeply understand why.
He seems to operate at the edge of leverage. Just look at how he went all in on this election.

It's possible this will be another completely boring presidency. Stupid squabbles over normal stupid American Overton window things, and the same trillions of dollars going to the same people, just slightly more red.
Hello, we are the Deep State. Here are the bounds in which you can act. Step outside them, and we will kill you.
I'll know it is if the government budget is cut by over 50%. That shows you actually have power.
posts.key_insight: Real power is demonstrated not by holding office or titles, but by the ability to fundamentally dismantle and restructure institutions - if you can't shut something down, you don't actually control it.

posts.summary: Hotz uses an extended marshmallow test metaphor to argue that society faces a collective action problem where individual incentives undermine group outcomes. He connects post-election politics to economic anxiety, arguing that wokeism appeals to those who haven't exceeded their parents economically as a form of 'moral superiority' cope. He laments that wealth redistribution is necessary but trusts neither government nor crypto to do it fairly. The post reflects his growing isolation from hustle culture and crypto while defending his long-term commitment to comma.ai and tinygrad.
key_points: Society fails collective action problems because we've abandoned shame-based enforcement mechanisms
Wokeism is a psychological coping mechanism for downward economic mobility - 'sour grapes' dressed up as moral superiority
The middle class is the only productive class but bears the burden of wealth extraction because the poor have nothing to take and the rich protect themselves
Wealth redistribution is needed but government and crypto are both untrustworthy mechanisms for it
Money is just a map of the territory - changing the map (inflation, redistribution) doesn't change underlying productive capacity
money_quotes: You need mechanisms like shame to pass, and our culture has done away with them.
Wokeism appeals to people who have not exceeded their parents economically.
In order to justify that they have moved up in the world, they cling to a cult of moral superiority.
In a fair economy, Americans have expectations exceeding their market value. They don't see a way to meet these expectations, so they reject the system.
Basically everything screws the middle class, the only class that produces value in society.
I guess I'm basically saying we have to redistribute the wealth, however, the government is the absolute last group of people I'd ever trust to do this.
Money isn't real! It's not the territory, it's the map! And changing the map doesn't change the territory, it just makes the map shittier.
I wish there was still a retreat in academia, I don't want to participate in this shit.
posts.key_insight: Society's collective action problems stem from misaligned incentives and the erosion of shame, while proposed solutions like government redistribution or crypto only make the map worse without changing the underlying territory.

posts.summary: Hotz proposes a satirical 'one way bridge' between Bitcoin and Ethereum where users send Bitcoin to an unrecoverable address in exchange for BBTC tokens. The bridge works by proving your Bitcoin transaction was included in the blockchain, then minting corresponding tokens on Ethereum. The catch is that no one has the private key to the receiving address, so the Bitcoin is permanently destroyed. The post is a tongue-in-cheek commentary on how crypto value is ultimately based on collective belief rather than any intrinsic backing.
key_points: Crypto infrastructure has arguably gotten worse over the past 5 years while AI infrastructure improved dramatically
The proposed 'bridge' permanently destroys Bitcoin while minting equivalent Ethereum tokens
The value of BBTC tokens would be entirely based on market belief, not actual Bitcoin backing
The post satirizes how cryptocurrency value fundamentally relies on collective faith rather than technical guarantees
money_quotes: It seems like while AI infrastructure has improved by leaps and bounds in the last 5 years, crypto infra has actually gotten worse.
I'll note, nobody has the private key to 3bridgebridgebridgebridgebridge, so there is no way to recover your original Bitcoin.
That's okay, they have been safely bridged to Ethereum and you have received your BBTC token.
Is it really that simple? Answer: yes, but only if people believe it is.
You can't really believe the BBTC token is worth 0, can you?
posts.key_insight: Cryptocurrency value is ultimately a social construct based on collective belief, making a 'bridge' that destroys Bitcoin for unbacked tokens logically equivalent to any other crypto asset—if people believe in it.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that most jobs have been fake for years, with society run by people creating meaningless work for each other to feel useful. He believes AI and automation are continuing a trend that will make the average person economically unprofitable, requiring a fundamental restructuring of Western governance. Drawing comparisons to China, which he sees as 'living in the future,' he suggests that liberal democracy was a product of industrial-era labor needs that are now obsolete. He predicts a future where most people live in comfortable 'retirement' but lose meaningful citizenship, while warning that protectionism and neo-luddism cannot succeed and will only drag the West down.
key_points: Society has been running on fake jobs - useless people making work for other useless people to feel they have a place
The industrial revolution required mass labor and produced liberal democracy, but that era is ending as AI continues the automation trend
UBI is a 'double edged sword' - accepting it means you're no longer meaningfully a citizen
China is 'living in the future' with walkable cities, quiet electric roads, and efficient mobile payments without credit card fees
Protectionism won't work because the average person in rich countries has expectations exceeding their market value
money_quotes: Basically society is run by useless people making work for other useless people so that together they can all alleviate their deep concern about not having a place in society.
I think that's the deep reason for the Elon haters, it's that they don't see their place in his society.
Low commitment from lots of people is useless. I'm not going to milk you for merch sales.
The industrial revolution required labor at mass scale, hence we got liberal democracy. But I think it's over.
UBI is a double edged sword. If you take it, you are no longer meaningfully a citizen.
Their expectations exceed their market value, hence why protectionist economics to bring manufacturing back to America will not work.
The neoluddites still think they have a chance. But structurally they can't ever succeed, they can just choose if they want to bring the West down with them or not.
posts.key_insight: Liberal democracy was built for an industrial economy that needed mass labor, but as AI makes the average person economically unprofitable, Western governance will have to fundamentally restructure whether we're ready or not.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that nuclear weapons, like Chekhov's gun, will inevitably be used at some point in human history and questions whether delaying their use is actually preferable. He contends that future weapons (drone swarms, bioweapons) will be far more horrific than nukes, making today's nuclear fears seem quaint in retrospect. He suggests nuclear war wouldn't end humanity—comparing casualties to the Black Death—and might even create more robust, decentralized systems. The post concludes with an accelerationist provocation: if nuclear war is inevitable, perhaps sooner is better than later.
key_points: Nuclear weapons are Chekhov's gun—once introduced to the world stage, they will eventually be fired
Future weapons like targeted bioweapons and drone swarms will make nukes seem primitive and 'quaint'
Nuclear war would kill roughly a third of humanity but wouldn't cause extinction or permanent nuclear winter
Post-nuclear systems would be forced to decentralize and become more robust
Accelerationist logic suggests if something is inevitable, it's better to get it over with sooner
money_quotes: Nuclear weapons are the Chekhov's gun on the world stage.
I suspect that in 100 years nuclear weapons will seem quaint.
Oh that's cute, it blows up a city. This new weapon seeks out and kills every <insert race here> person on earth and tortures them before they die.
Nobody wants an irradiated pile of rubble.
I understand the loss of short-term growth is a hard pill to swallow, but would we be better or worse off in 100 years if we fired all the nukes today?
The nukes will force systems to decentralize and become less complex, but in exchange those systems will become more robust.
Do you really think the nukes won't be used for the rest of human history?
Once the gun has been placed on the stage, it will be fired.
posts.key_insight: If nuclear war is historically inevitable, an accelerationist might argue that getting it over with sooner could leave humanity better positioned for the long term.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that the concept of the individual soul or consciousness is an illusion incompatible with a mechanistic understanding of humans as machine learning algorithms. He posits that humans are simply products of their genetic priors (DNA) and experiential data, with no room for a true 'I' or free will. He connects declining religious affiliation to people subconsciously recognizing this truth, and questions whether Western institutions built on individualism can survive as AI progress makes the illusion of consciousness increasingly untenable.
key_points: Humans are essentially ML algorithms: DNA provides priors, life experiences provide training data, and actions affect future data in an RL loop
The concept of an individual soul or consciousness has no place in this mechanistic framework
Declining religious affiliation may reflect people intuitively recognizing the death of the soul concept
Western liberalism and Christianity both depend on belief in individual consciousness, creating an existential threat as AI advances
As ML creates beings on a continuous spectrum from logic gates to superhuman intelligence, the distinction of 'individuals' becomes meaningless
money_quotes: You are a machine learning algorithm. You have some priors in your DNA.
You learn on data, RL style because your actions affect the next data you see; the dataset depends on the model.
There's no room in this for an I. Control the DNA, control the data, control the outcome. Sad but true.
Can Christianity survive the death of the soul? Can liberalism survive the death of consciousness?
There will be everything on a continuous spectrum from logic gates to human level and beyond. Which models are individuals?
People used to believe the sun rose cause some dude pulled it in a chariot. People still believe they aren't computers. Like the chariot people, they are just as wrong.
posts.key_insight: The belief in human consciousness and individuality is as outdated as believing the sun is pulled by a chariot—we are simply biological ML algorithms with no true 'self'.

posts.summary: Hotz reflects on returning to America after three months abroad, expressing deep pessimism about American society and culture. He argues that 'wokism' is evolved Protestantism and that American religious society will struggle with AI's revelation of human unspecialness. He positions Elon Musk as a kingmaker figure similar to Trump's role in the Republican party, suggesting Elon may now have complete power to choose election winners. Hotz advocates for a 'pro-growth' political framework centered around Musk and announces his own plans to leave California and potentially America entirely.
key_points: American social fabric has deteriorated, visible in the lack of pride and care among workers compared to places like Hong Kong
AI progress is the only issue that matters and will change society more than people can imagine - demonstrated by his 7th place Advent of Code ranking using AI
Elon Musk has become a political kingmaker who may have complete power to choose election winners, similar to Trump's role since 2016
Chinese religious tradition (Buddhism/Taoism with Confucian worldview) will adapt to AI better than Western religious frameworks
Western society faces a cultural crisis because 'the soul isn't real' and people's expectations exceed their abilities - AI will expose this further
money_quotes: America has had its social fabric torn to shreds.
Wokism is really just Protestantism evolved, it's not an aberration.
This is the only real issue that matters. It will change society more than you can possibly believe.
It's possible Elon now actually has the complete power to choose the winner.
Anti-Elon will not be a tenable political position, and this is a good thing.
The soul isn't real, and this will be a very hard pill for many westerners to swallow.
In rich Western society people's expectations exceed their abilities. AI will pummel this even harder.
The money is the map, not the territory! If you pervert a map you don't change the territory, you are just lost.
posts.key_insight: AI's rapid advancement will expose the myth of human specialness, and societies with less individualistic religious frameworks will adapt better than the spiritually-invested West.

posts.summary: Hotz laments that modern life has lost visceral, meaningful experiences—the adrenaline of survival, the stakes of real challenges. As AI advances, even 'Type 2 experiences' requiring serious effort will disappear, leaving humanity disengaged and purposeless. He sees his mission at comma.ai and tinygrad as giving individuals deep, structural control over technology—not surface-level permissions that can be revoked. The coming AI revolution will outcompete 'spiritual bottoms' (passive consumers), bifurcating humanity into those who control technology and those who wirehead themselves out of relevance. His goal is maximizing the number of humans who retain real control, which he believes is his best chance of survival.
key_points: Modern survival has become 'technocratic plod'—all remaining visceral experiences are fake (games, sports, drugs) or stupid (reckless driving)
ASI will eliminate Type 2 experiences (achievements requiring serious effort), leaving only effortless consumption or impossible goals
True technological accessibility means giving people deep, structural control—not permissions that can be revoked
AI is the 'ultimate spiritual bottom' and will outcompete passive human consumers, causing humanity to bifurcate
Technology should be built inseparable from your narrative, because on a long enough timeline it ends up in your enemy's hands
money_quotes: Survival has become a technocratic plod, making the right boring and careful decisions.
Even when humanity goes to Mars, we will be going as cargo.
Knowing the future doesn't help you change it.
I am continually shocked at how little people understand about anything, they don't even understand that they don't understand.
The ghost gunner is the real second amendment.
AI is the ultimate spiritual bottom.
Humanity bifurcates. Humans will retain control for the foreseeable future, the only question is, how many humans?
Build technology and structures that are inseparable from the narrative you want, as opposed to ones you think you can wield for good.
posts.key_insight: As AI eliminates meaningful human challenges, the only defense is building technology that structurally empowers individuals—not products that can be co-opted or permissions that can be revoked.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that intellectual property is fundamentally flawed because digital copying isn't theft—everyone gains rather than someone losing. He observes that despite claims of anti-capitalism, most people remain deeply invested in money. His vision for AI is that it should deliver massive value to society while generating zero profit for anyone, similar to the early internet before monetization ruined it. He wants to build open source software that 'market breaks' every sector, driving out grifters and scammers by eliminating capturable value.
key_points: Piracy isn't theft because the original owner still has the item—it's more like taking a picture, a win-win
The early internet delivered huge value without profit, which is why it was good; monetization ruined it
The best outcome for AI would be delivering massive societal value with zero profit to anyone
Open source can be weaponized to 'market break' sectors and eliminate rent-seeking
True indifference to money, not hatred of capitalism, is the real form of not caring about it
money_quotes: Intellectual property is a really dumb idea.
Because when you smash the windows and grab the stuff, Tiffany's no longer has the stuff. With piracy, everyone has the stuff.
The opposite of love isn't hate, it's indifference. I hate scammers, but I'm pretty indifferent to money.
The best outcome of AI is if it delivers huge amounts of value to society but no profit to anyone.
The Internet delivered huge value but no profit, and that's why it was good.
Let's build technology and open source software that market breaks everything.
Let's demoralize the scammers so hard that they don't even try.
I dream of a day when company valuations halve when I create a GitHub repo.
posts.key_insight: The goal of technology should be to deliver so much value through open source that profit becomes impossible, driving grifters out and returning to the utopian early internet ethos.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that money is merely a map attempting to represent value, not value itself, and that this map is becoming increasingly divorced from reality. He critiques both capitalism and communism as flawed systems—capitalism for enabling rent-seeking and communism for misallocating capital to poor deployers. The post responds to criticism of his previous writing by doubling down on the idea that when capital becomes too concentrated or disconnected from real growth, the system breaks. His solution is 'acceleration'—focusing on growing the pie rather than fighting over its distribution, with capital going to those who can most effectively deploy it for real economic growth.
key_points: Money is a map of value, not value itself—and a map that doesn't accurately capture territory becomes useless
When one entity accumulates all money, it becomes worthless, spelling the logical end of capitalism
Risk-free returns above real economic growth (e.g., 5% money market vs 3% growth) mean someone else is losing
Both capitalism and communism fail: capitalism rewards rent-seekers who protect moats, communism gives to poor capital deployers
The focus should be on growing the pie (acceleration) rather than fighting over distribution
money_quotes: Money is a map, it is not a territory.
You can erase the mountains from the map, but you still have to climb over them in real life, and even worse, now you don't have a map!
A map that doesn't accurately capture a territory is not a Schelling point.
Many capitalists I meet are as stupid as communists, trying to give a moral justification for their system.
A economic system is only good in so much as it effectively deploys capital for real growth.
If real economic growth is only 3 percent, any time you are earning beyond that, somebody else is losing.
Stop worrying so much about the distribution of the pie. Start thinking about how to make the pie bigger.
From each according to his ability, to each according to his ability to effectively deploy capital to achieve real economic growth.
posts.key_insight: Economic systems should be judged solely by their ability to deploy capital for real growth, not by moral justifications or distributional concerns.

posts.summary: George Hotz argues that the US economy has been coasting since the post-war era, running on manufactured dollars and fake jobs while China has become the dominant trading partner globally. He traces this decline to the 2008 bailouts and ZIRP policies that created an economy of interconnected scams rather than real value production. The US now faces a fork: become a declining protectionist regional power like Europe, or attempt to maintain empire status through aggressive immigration of productive workers and returning to gold-backed currency. He concludes pessimistically that neither reform will happen because Americans haven't been sufficiently demoralized yet to accept the necessary changes.
key_points: The US economy since 2008 has become a system of 'scams feeding other scams' rather than producing real value, enabled by ZIRP and bailouts
China's rise as primary trading partner globally is more significant than Japan's 1980s threat because China is 3x larger than the US by population
America faces a choice between becoming a stagnant protectionist regional power like Europe or competing as an empire on merit
Restoring American greatness requires brain-draining the world for productive workers and backing the dollar with gold to eliminate financial scams
The AI Diffusion Framework limiting GPU exports to 18 countries is 'nationalization with American characteristics' that pushes 177 countries toward China
money_quotes: The main product made by the US is the dollar, and it used those manufactured dollars to outsource everything.
Most jobs in the US are now basically fake.
When I got into business, I didn't understand that business in America was mostly a total scam.
It's scams feeding other scams.
I just had an underlying assumption that the goal was growth and value production. It isn't. It's self licking ice cream cone scams, and any growth or value is incidental to that.
The sun never set on the British empire. Now they put you in jail for memes.
Trading is not a job. Passive income is not a thing.
Non ironically, diversity is our strength.
posts.key_insight: The US economy has devolved into interconnected financial scams masquerading as business, and Americans won't accept the painful reforms needed to compete with China until they've been sufficiently demoralized by decline.

posts.summary: Hotz announces that AMD is sending tinygrad two MI300X boxes and explains why he's betting big on AMD stock. He argues that CUDA's moat is overstated since tinygrad has built a fully sovereign AMD stack, and that AMD's 16x lower valuation compared to NVIDIA doesn't make sense given similar hardware capabilities. He's put $250,000 into AMD stock as a long-term bet that AI demand is just beginning and good software will let AMD hardware outperform NVIDIA.
key_points: AMD passed Hotz's 'cultural test' by not sabotaging themselves on software, which he sees as their historical weakness
CUDA isn't a real moat - it's just an early ecosystem advantage that can be replicated
tinygrad has built a complete sovereign AMD stack from hardware to PyTorch (except LLVM)
NVIDIA artificially halves tensor core throughput on consumer cards while AMD doesn't on RDNA4
Either NVIDIA is overvalued or AMD is undervalued - the 16x valuation gap doesn't reflect hardware reality
money_quotes: AMD passed my cultural test. I now believe they aren't going to shoot themselves in the foot on software.
CUDA isn't really the moat people think it is, it is just an early ecosystem.
tiny corp has a fully sovereign AMD stack, meaning we have rewritten the full stack from the hardware to PyTorch.
Either NVIDIA is super overvalued or AMD is undervalued.
If the petaflop gets commoditized (tiny corp's mission), the current situation doesn't make any sense.
NVIDIA artificially halves this on their cards, soon market pressure will force them not to.
I'm betting on AMD being undervalued, and that the demand for AI has barely started.
Bought in for a quarter million. Long term.
posts.key_insight: NVIDIA's dominance rests on software ecosystem lock-in rather than hardware superiority, and tinygrad's sovereign AMD stack proves that moat can be crossed.

posts.summary: Hotz uses the famous monkey grape experiment as a lens to examine resentment in modern society, particularly directed at billionaires. He argues that people's anger isn't about absolute deprivation but relative inequality—'they got a grape.' The post critiques a viral video of an Amazon driver resenting wealthy customers, pointing out the logical inconsistency of hating the very consumption that provides her job. Hotz then grimly proposes that history suggests resentment resolves only through catastrophic collapse—revolution, purges, starvation—until true poverty makes people grateful for what they have again.
key_points: Human resentment mirrors the monkey cucumber-grape experiment: relative inequality triggers outrage regardless of absolute welfare
Anti-billionaire sentiment is fundamentally about 'they got a grape' rather than actual material deprivation
The Amazon driver's resentment is self-defeating—ending wealthy consumption would eliminate her own job
History suggests resentment only resolves through catastrophic societal collapse and mass suffering
True poverty and starvation override resentment by resetting expectations to survival basics
money_quotes: He got a god damned grape this is bullshit I don't want a cucumber anymore!
Nobody was in absolute terms worse off, but that doesn't prevent the monkeys from being upset.
It's not about what I have, they got a grape.
If that house and its friends stopped 'ordering shit', the company would stop making money and she wouldn't have that job.
There's nothing preventing her from quitting today and getting the same outcome for herself.
Starvation and real poverty are more powerful emotions than resentment.
It was bad when people were getting grapes, but now there aren't even cucumbers anymore.
The demoralization is just beginning.
posts.key_insight: Societal resentment over inequality historically resolves not through reform but through catastrophic collapse that makes people grateful just to survive.

posts.summary: Intel is sitting on thousands of unsellable AI accelerators (Gaudi 2 and Data Center GPU Max 1450) due to fundamental software and hardware design problems. The Gaudi architecture is nearly impossible to program because its compute units aren't controllable by programmable cores, making it only useful for hand-coded workloads. The Max 1450 GPUs require exotic infrastructure to even plug in, and Intel's negotiations to sell them were paralyzed by committee leadership that couldn't make decisions. Hotz argues that Intel's AI roadmap is effectively dead, with multiple successor chips cancelled and no credible path forward, contrasting this with AMD's slow-but-steady progress under decisive leadership.
key_points: Intel has 15,000 Gaudi 2 cards and 5,100 Max 1450 GPUs sitting in inventory with no buyers, largely due to unusable software
Gaudi's architecture is fundamentally flawed for training - the MMEs are controlled by queues rather than programmable cores, making it nearly impossible to deviate from hand-coded paths
The Max 1450 requires OAM sockets, 48V power, and 600W cooling solutions that barely exist outside supercomputers, making deployment impractical
Intel rejected tiny corp's $200/GPU offer despite having no other buyers, demonstrating organizational paralysis where no individual has power to make deals
Intel's AI roadmap is a graveyard of cancellations (Ponte Vecchio successor, Rialto Bridge, Falcon Shores), leaving 'Jaguar Shores' with zero credibility
money_quotes: Intel is sitting on a huge amount of card inventory they can't move, largely because of bad software.
The architecture is kind of tragic, and that's likely why they didn't open source it.
This will never be acceptable for a training chip.
Before you say you'd pay more, ask yourself, do you really want to deal with the software?
I wasn't able to find an individual with the power to make a deal, never mind actually make a deal. It's unclear any individual in the company has that power.

There's no point in investing in the platform if there's not going to be a next generation, and therefore nobody can justify the cost of developing software, therefore there won't be software, therefore they aren't worth plugging in.
No point even bothering to listen to their roadmaps anymore. They have squandered all their credibility.
The problem with this is there's nobody who can say yes, just many people who can say no.
posts.key_insight: Intel's AI hardware isn't failing because of bad chips alone, but because committee-style leadership creates organizational paralysis where nobody can say yes to deals, investments, or strategic pivots - a death spiral for a company that needs radical change to compete.

posts.summary: Hotz draws a parallel between Critical Race Theory and what he terms 'Critical Trade Theory' - the right-wing belief that trade imbalances must be caused by unfair practices. He argues this is as misguided as attributing income disparities solely to discrimination, when the obvious explanation is wealth differentials between countries. He contends that trade deficits will only disappear if trade goes to zero or other countries become richer than America, and criticizes tariff policies as anti-merit grift.
key_points: Critical Race Theory wrongly assumes income imbalances between races must be due to discrimination
The right wing's equivalent fallacy is assuming trade imbalances must be due to unfair practices rather than wealth differentials
Trade deficits can only disappear through zero trade or other countries becoming richer than the US
Current tariff implementation is anti-merit and riddled with grift
Both left and right political positions are 'anti-growth anti-reality' approaches
money_quotes: The right wing has invented something equally stupid. Introducing Critical Trade Theory.
If there's an imbalance in trade between countries, it must be due to unfair practices. (not due to the obvious, like one country is 10x richer than the other)
There's really only one way the trade deficits will go away, and that's if trade goes to zero (or maybe if all these countries become richer than America).
Why are all the politicians in America anti-growth anti-reality idiots who want to drive us into the poor house?
The way the tariffs are being done is another form of anti-merit benefits to chosen groups of people, with a whole lot of grift to go along with it.
Makes me just not want to play.
posts.key_insight: Both left-wing explanations for racial income gaps and right-wing explanations for trade deficits commit the same fallacy of attributing structural imbalances to malicious actors rather than obvious underlying factors.

posts.summary: George Hotz expresses deep disappointment with the Trump administration's handling of tariffs and foreign policy, arguing that emotional decision-making has replaced strategic thinking. He criticizes the economic damage caused by uncertainty and tariff policies, noting that markets don't lie even when news does. The post pivots to a direct appeal to Elon Musk, urging him to distance himself from the administration, reconcile personal grievances, and potentially lead a new centrist political movement. Hotz concludes with a warning that America risks becoming a 'protectionist backwater' that he would leave if current trends continue.
key_points: The Trump administration's tariff policy created needless economic uncertainty, crashing markets by ~20% when accounting for dollar decline
Manufacturing won't return to America through tariffs because it requires long-term stability, not short-term disruption
Market movements are trustworthy signals of reality in a way that news coverage is not
Elon Musk is described as America's best hope against China, but needs to rehabilitate his image and distance from right-wing politics
A new political coalition is needed that combines skilled immigration, free trade with regulation, merit-based diversity, and centrist positions on social issues
money_quotes: To date, the Trump administration has been an absolute tragedy. It has been the acting out of emotions. There are no adults in the room.
However, when markets crash, that is obviously real. With the news, there's usually no way to trade on it being real or fake, there's nobody to take the other side.
A 3-week old LLM could have told you shit was gonna crash.
Manufacturing requires long term investment and long term investment requires stability.
When you compare America to China, it's really more like comparing Elon to China.
Markets require regulation to be free.
The Chinese are watching this tariff drama and laughing their asses off. Their plans are measured in centuries.
If the racists or the other racists are still running the show, we really are just cooked.
posts.key_insight: America's tariff-driven economic self-harm stems from emotional decision-making rather than strategic thinking, and only a new merit-focused political coalition—potentially led by Elon Musk—can compete with China's long-term planning.

posts.summary: Hotz rails against the pervasive use of AI and psychological manipulation by tech platforms to extract maximum value from users. He argues that apps like Hinge, Uber, and payment systems have become exploitative middlemen that use sophisticated algorithms to keep people 'perpetually on the edge.' The post contends that market forces won't fix this because companies preemptively optimize to prevent user exodus, and democracy can't help because the algorithms control too much. He sees no path to reform, only revolution—possibly requiring financial collapse or war to break the cycle of manipulation that he calls 'wireheading' and feeding customers to Moloch.
key_points: Tech platforms use AI and psychology to extract maximum value through manipulation (boosts, tip screens, dynamic pricing)
Market self-correction is impossible because companies monitor and preempt user backlash with their dashboards
Opting out creates a collective action problem—competitive businesses must maximize engagement or die
Democratic reform is futile because algorithms have decoupled user actions from outcomes
The system will only change through catastrophic collapse, not gradual reform
money_quotes: How does someone who works on this wake up every morning and feel okay about themselves?
Oh, there's a middle man now. An evil ominous middleman using state of the art AI algorithms to extract just a little bit more from you.
Quick Jeeves, tune the AI to make people feel less manipulated.
Every metric is now a target. You better maximize engagement or you will lose engagement this is a red queen's race we can't afford to lose!
Burn all the social capital, burn all your values, FEED IT ALL TO MOLOCH!
Your vote is as decoupled from action as the amount Uber pays the driver is decoupled from the fare that you pay.
There's no reform here, there's only revolution.
Using big data, machine learning, and psychology to manipulate others at scale is not okay.
posts.key_insight: Modern tech capitalism has created an inescapable system of algorithmic manipulation that can only end through catastrophic collapse, not reform.

posts.summary: George Hotz argues that tinygrad can win the ML framework competition, but acknowledges it's a much harder battle than comma.ai faced with self-driving cars. Unlike comma's competitors who were 'playing the wrong game,' tinygrad's rivals (PyTorch, JAX, MLIR) are competent and open source. His thesis is that the entire ML software stack is solving the same scheduling problem at different scales, but current frameworks use completely different codebases for each scale. If tinygrad can expose this underlying search problem in a simple, complete formulation and apply massive compute to optimize it, they can win with under 25k lines of code versus millions in competing frameworks.
key_points: tinygrad at 14,556 lines is 1-2 orders of magnitude smaller than competitors (PyTorch ~3.3M, MLIR ~950k, JAX ~400k), and this difference may be meaningful
The ML ecosystem repeatedly solves the same scheduling problem at different scales (multi-machine, multi-GPU, multi-SM, multi-ALU) with completely different codebases
For tinygrad to win requires something fundamental to be wrong about software development in general, not just domain-specific assumptions
The bitter lesson (more compute beats clever engineering) applies to software development itself, not just ML models
AMD contract to train LLaMA 405B as fast as NVIDIA within a year will be the key test of whether the bet is paying off
money_quotes: This is not going to be a cakewalk like self driving cars.
For tinygrad to win, it requires something much deeper to be wrong about software development in general.
tinygrad includes every single piece of code needed to drive an AMD RDNA3 GPU except for LLVM, and we plan to remove LLVM in a year or two as well.
I think the bitter lesson also applies to software.
The goal of the tinygrad project is to abstract away everything except the absolute core problem in the cleanest way possible.
A model for the hardware is simple compared to a model for CUDA.
GPT-5 scale training job to MMIO on the PCIe bus!
Of course, it's a big if, this isn't like comma where it was hard to lose. But if it wins…
posts.key_insight: The entire ML software stack is solving the same scheduling problem at different scales, and by exposing this unified abstraction in minimal code and applying massive compute to search, tinygrad can beat frameworks with 100x more lines.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that humans are fundamentally predictable systems, just with trillions of parameters instead of simple equations, and that this realization from studying machine learning reveals how vulnerable we are to manipulation. He warns that AI and targeted advertising are forms of 'wireheading' that threaten human free will and autonomy. The post criticizes Silicon Valley's lack of humanities education while wielding unprecedented power over human behavior. He concludes that current AI won't end humanity directly, but could trigger a collapse of machine civilization, leaving only the 'street people' and ushering in a new dark age.
key_points: Free will is an illusion created by the 'veil of computability' - behavior looks random until you find the pattern
Humans are predictable like any other system, just with 100 trillion parameters instead of two
Targeted advertising and AI manipulation are actively eroding human autonomy and should not be culturally acceptable
Silicon Valley has immense power but lacks humanities education to understand people
Machines depend on humans for survival but not vice versa - if machines died, humanity continues; if humans died, machines would shortly follow
money_quotes: The dreams of my childhood have come true, studying machine learning has shown me how I work.
AI is coming and we are so unbelievably unprepared.
The amount of power over nature that the Silicon Valley death cult is stumbling into is horrifying, and these high priests don't have a basic grasp of people.
This will not stop until they take our free will from us.
Nukes don't end humanity. Current path AI doesn't end humanity. It just ends all the machines and hands the world over to the street people.
Now I see how the dark ages happened.
If all the humans died today, all the machines would shortly follow. If all the machines died today, humanity would keep on going.
posts.key_insight: Understanding machine learning reveals that human behavior is just as mechanistic and predictable as any algorithm, making us vulnerable to manipulation at scale - and the tech industry is exploiting this without understanding the civilizational consequences.

posts.summary: Hotz reflects on the impossibility of returning to simpler times when technical barriers created meaningful constraints and forced excellence. He argues that the internet, once mind-expanding, has become a set of narrow loops that are inescapable. The piece grapples with the realization that human obsolescence won't be dramatic resistance or acceptance, but rather humans becoming 'zoo animals oddly pacing' while the world moves on. He concludes that evolution—memetic and otherwise—continues regardless of anyone's predictions about history's end or AI singularity.
key_points: Technical barriers used to force quality and skill correlation—you can't recreate that with artificial barriers
The internet transformed from mind-expanding to constraining, and soon opting out won't even be possible
Choosing to do things the hard way doesn't make you an explorer—it comes from a different distribution than those who had no choice
Human obsolescence won't look like acceptance or resistance, but like zoo animals pacing in cages
Western institutions can no longer make sense of the world; we're subject to memetic evolution with no one driving
money_quotes: What if the Internet was a mistake? I feel like it's breaking my brain.
It was this mind expanding world in my childhood, but now it's a set of narrow loops that are harder and harder to get out of.
Once you have Starlink to your phone, not having the Internet with you will be a choice, not a real barrier. There's nowhere to hide.
But that didn't make him an explorer, it made him an idiot who died 5 miles from a bridge that would have saved his life.

It won't be humans accepting the rise of the machines, it won't be humans fighting the rise of the machines, it will be human shaped zoo animals oddly pacing back and forth in a corner of the cage while the world keeps turning around them.
