Even if they hate you, it's more comforting to believe that they exist. That at least somebody is driving. But that's not true. It's just going.
The TikTok brainrot kids will be nothing compared to the ChatGPT brainrot kids.
To every fool preaching the end of history, evolution spits in your face.
posts.key_insight: You can't recreate the conditions that made constraints meaningful—choosing limitation isn't the same as having no choice, and humanity's fate isn't dramatic obsolescence but becoming confused animals in a world that no longer needs our participation.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that society misdirects its anger at billionaires and immigrants when the real problem is unproductive people across all economic strata. He contends that both groups being scapegoated are actually productive contributors to America. The true villains are rent-seekers, obstructionists, and those with 'fake jobs' who create complexity without adding value—whether rich or poor. He predicts that targeting billionaires and immigrants will only make problems worse while the actual causes remain unaddressed.
key_points: Both billionaires and immigrants are productive groups being unfairly scapegoated
The real problem is unproductive people at all economic levels—from finance middlemen to welfare recipients
Rent-seeking, obstruction, and artificial complexity creation are the true drags on society
Current political discourse targets the wrong groups while protecting the actual problem
Zero-sum thinking and broken systems elevate people who don't create value
money_quotes: It's going to be so painful to watch. The tearing down of two of the productive groups in America.
Billionaires will go away. Immigrants will go away. The problems will continue to get worse.
The problem is the unproductive. The rich unproductive and the poor unproductive.
The finance middle-man and welfare recipient. The real estate agent and the person on disability. The person with a fake job.
Anyone who purposefully creates complexity for others. The obstructionists. Rent seekers.
Broken systems that elevate zero sum losers.
I don't know why most people don't see it.
posts.key_insight: Society's problems stem not from wealth or immigration but from unproductive rent-seekers and complexity-creators at every economic level who extract value without creating it.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that being a 'good person' means producing more than you consume over your lifetime. He draws a 'bright line' to prevent purity spirals when critiquing the unproductive, emphasizing that the metric is net lifetime production, not maximizing output at every moment. He identifies the 'unproductive rich' (private equity, lawyers, lobbyists) as the real problem, claiming they ally with the unproductive poor to extract value from productive people. The post calls for a cultural shift to elevate value-creating jobs while making 'negative sum jobs' socially unacceptable.
key_points: The moral standard for being a 'good person' is simple: produce more than you consume over your lifetime
The unproductive rich are worse than the unproductive poor because they frame extraction as moral charity while being 'unproductive losers' themselves
Jobs like private equity, real estate, sales, lawyers, and lobbyists are 'negative sum' and should become socially unacceptable
Productive work spans from billionaires to fry cooks—the key is creating tangible value, not income level
Democracy itself depends on the average person being net positive; if they aren't, 'we're already too far gone'
money_quotes: But at the end of your life, total it all up. You should have produced more than you consumed. That's what it means to be a good person.
The unproductive rich are in cahoots with the unproductive poor to take from you.
They loudly frame helping the unproductive as a moral issue for helping the poor because they know deep down they are unproductive losers.
Private equity, market manipulators, real estate, sales, lawyers, lobbyists. This is no longer okay.
From productive billionaires to the fry cook at McDonalds. They are both good people.
The days of living off the work of everyone else are over. We live in a society.
Whenever you decide to turn against a group, it's very important to prevent purity spirals.
posts.key_insight: Moral worth should be measured by net lifetime production versus consumption, and society must culturally reject extractive professions regardless of their wealth or status.

posts.summary: This is the opening chapter of a dystopian fiction piece set in a near-future where young people have become passive, asexual consumers glued to their phones. The protagonist John, a 19-year-old, reflects on his absent father, his aimless generation, and the sanitized internet before being interrupted by police detectives leaving a cryptic message. The story establishes a world where algorithms have neutered human desire and curiosity, redirecting people away from anything not 'advertiser friendly.' John's father abandoned Wall Street for an off-grid cabin filled with mysterious lab equipment, hinting at larger forces at play.
key_points: Depicts a generation that has lost interest in sex and human connection, reduced to passive scrolling and consumption
The internet has been fully sanitized by algorithms that don't censor but redirect, making transgression nearly impossible
John's father had a 'crisis of conscience' about capitalism and now lives off-grid with suspicious lab equipment
The story contrasts comfort-seeking algorithmic consumption with the anxiety of real-world intrusion
Sets up a mystery involving police interest in John, likely connected to his father's activities
money_quotes: It seemed like the rest of my generation was no longer interested in sex.
They were the agendered mass like the rest of us. Fellow consumers, not providers.
It wasn't actually censored, it would just redirect you elsewhere. And if you didn't pay careful attention, you wouldn't even notice it happening.
Once the algorithms got good enough and it was technically easy to censor, there was nothing holding them back.
I tried asking ChatGPT about hookers and it told me to call them sex workers. And this was kind of triggering. Who the fuck does this machine think it is?
The algorithms got a rise out of me and I went back to comfort food YouTube.
He wanted to talk about 'man shit' like nature and women and not being life's bitch.
posts.key_insight: When algorithms become sophisticated enough to seamlessly redirect rather than overtly censor, they don't just control information—they extinguish desire itself.

posts.summary: This is a fictional narrative chapter following John and his friends Brian and Dave at a bagel shop the morning after a night of smoking weed. The story reveals backstory about Dave's brother's suicide and its devastating effect on his family, while John realizes that cops who visited him were actually looking for his father, not him. The chapter establishes the dynamics between three high school friends - Brian the crass but loyal one, Dave the traumatized stoner, and John the narrator with an absent, possibly troubled father. The tone blends mundane teenage life with darker undercurrents of family tragedy and mystery.
key_points: Dave's older brother committed suicide when they were freshmen, leading Dave to cope through heavy marijuana use while his mother became a recluse and his sister fled to California
Brian is characterized as a Stifler-type who uses his mom's credit card (from a settlement) while making friends Venmo him - simultaneously generous and scamming
John realizes the cops were looking for his father Jonathan, not him, connecting to his dad's previous workplace where Dave's brother also worked
The father is described as a 'Kaczynski freak' who 'bugged out,' suggesting he may have done something radical or criminal
The chapter hints at something sinister about the workplace that employed both John's father and Dave's deceased brother
money_quotes: He called himself a gentleman, but not in the creepy Elliot Rodger way, or in anything resembling the real meaning of the word.
His mom's credit card and we Venmo him. That's the type of guy he was.
Like we would smoke and get high, but for Dave it was a different thing. He would smoke till he was catatonic.
He graduated valedictorian and she never did. Maybe that wasn't enough?
Dave would go to dark places and Brian couldn't be brought there. So it worked.
Last night I thought my world was closing in, now I realize this just all isn't my problem.
At least my dad isn't some kind of Kaczynski freak.
posts.key_insight: This fictional chapter explores how teenage friendships form around shared brokenness - absent fathers, family tragedy, and coping mechanisms - while hinting at a larger mystery connecting the characters' families.

posts.summary: This fictional narrative follows Jonathan, a brilliant quantitative trader at Operant Capital, who has been leading the RPS (rock-paper-scissors) project—an attempt to predict human choices before they're made. The research builds on neuroscience findings that brain scans can detect decisions before conscious awareness. After years of incremental progress reaching 54% first-round accuracy, Jonathan returns from a weekend away to discover his team has achieved something far more disturbing: perfect prediction of his entire sequence of moves, including when he would leave the room, all computed before he played a single game.
key_points: Markets are fundamentally different from physics—they're emergent behavior from billions of agents predicting each other's predictions
The RPS project explores whether human decisions can be predicted from external signals like social media posts or facial features
Early neuroscience research showed MRI machines could detect button-press decisions before subjects were consciously aware of their choice
The breakthrough isn't just prediction—it's that feedback becomes unnecessary when predictions are known to be correct
The story raises questions about free will and determinism through a near-future financial technology lens
money_quotes: The market moves based on the thoughts and feelings of billions of humans, and they were all trying to do this same prediction thing.
This was a Red Queen's race, where the tactics that worked last year didn't work this year. Everyone was predicting everyone else predicting the market.
Technical analysis is complete bullshit. This is a simple betting game played against other agents.
But what was crazier is that the machine knew which button you would press before you were even aware of your choice.
Maybe you had to look inside to spot a scissors type guy.
If it knows all the predictions are going to be correct, why does it need any feedback?
posts.key_insight: If prediction becomes perfect, the distinction between foreknowledge and determinism collapses—free will becomes an illusion we haven't yet instrumented away.

posts.summary: This is a fictional narrative chapter following a protagonist who finds a detective's business card at his door and spirals into paranoia about why Nassau County police would visit his Brooklyn home. Despite not having committed any crime, he obsesses over police interrogation tactics and surveillance possibilities. He eventually calms himself, attributing the visit to his father's business dealings, and goes to a friend's basement party where they smoke weed and watch videos. The chapter explores the psychological weight of unexpected police contact and the comfort found in mundane social rituals.
key_points: The narrator applies true crime knowledge about not talking to police while recognizing his own vulnerability to manipulation
Paranoia feeds on itself: not knowing why police visited is worse than knowing you committed a crime
The chapter contrasts surveillance anxiety with the grounding normalcy of friendship and teenage rituals
A stoner's 'what if we're the mouse' observation gets dismissed, but the narrator's paranoia suggests he feels exactly like that mouse
Physical security rituals (three locks) provide psychological comfort against abstract threats
money_quotes: I had watched enough true crime to know that you should never talk to the police. And I wasn't arrogant enough to believe that I was different.
Another system carefully crafted based on years and years of 'user feedback' designed to manipulate you into doing what it wants.
If I did commit a crime this would all be a lot easier, I'd know why they were here and what they wanted and could plan my next move accordingly.
From both a fear of monitored communication and a fear of spreading my paranoia, this wasn't worth texting her about.
Even though I couldn't see it, it reminded me that there was a society out there.
With the blanket of those three locks, a bunch of beers, and a couple hits of the Volcano, drifted off to sleep.
posts.key_insight: The absence of guilt doesn't protect you from the psychological weight of institutional power - uncertainty about why you're being watched can be more destabilizing than knowing you've done something wrong.

posts.summary: This chapter follows the protagonist investigating their mother's disappearance while uncovering family secrets. After a conversation with Dave about their fathers both working at the mysterious 'Operant' company, the narrator searches through their mother's dresser drawers. They discover a document showing their mother receives $10,000 monthly from something called the Triangle Trust. When they can't reach their mother and try to track her car through the Mercedes app, they find the account has been changed to an unknown email address, 'skinner666@gmail.com', suggesting something sinister is happening.
key_points: Both Dave's father and someone named Tom worked at 'Operant' and both apparently 'went crazy' - suggesting the company may be connected to psychological damage
The protagonist's family went from wealthy (yacht in Monaco, Cobble Hill mansion) to middle-class (Sheepshead Bay) after their father left, but money kept coming through the Triangle Trust
The narrator admits true crime podcasts have 'done bad things' for their personality and skewed their view of people
The mother's car tracking account has been changed to an unknown email 'skinner666@gmail.com' - a clear sign of foul play or deception
The parents never officially divorced - the father 'just left' but continued financial support
money_quotes: Resolving to be less of a pussy, I answered it.
It was always strange to me that that generation would just drop by. Like she didn't text her first?
I know that the people on there are out of the normal distribution of people, but those podcasts are one of my only exposures to the outside world.
I'd always just assumed my Dad was like, a Wall Street guy. Boring. Get money, fuck bitches.
My mom loved the luxury lifestyle, and would put up with a lot of my Dad's eccentricities to keep it.
As far as I knew, they never got a divorce. He just left.
A jolt of anxiety coarsed through my body. I'd never seen that e-mail address in my life.
posts.key_insight: The mundane act of searching through a parent's belongings reveals how little we know about the people closest to us and the secrets that structure our lives.

posts.summary: This speculative fiction piece follows Detective James Reese investigating Operant, a tech company he suspects of using subliminal environmental manipulation to control behavior. The investigation centers on Tom Park, a researcher who died falling from Operant's roof under suspicious circumstances—his phone records show he was home, but security footage shows a silhouette on the roof. Reese discovers the company uses precisely timed environmental cues (lighting, vibrations, HVAC sounds, elevator movements) to nudge people's decisions without their awareness. A leaked issue tracker reveals engineers debating whether their 'prediction' system is actually causing the outcomes it claims to predict. The detective concludes that the real sophistication isn't the technology itself, but the careful bookkeeping that makes everything look normal and leaves nothing illegal in the logs.
key_points: The story reframes 'mind control' as 'nudge'—precisely timed environmental cues that make decisions feel self-generated rather than externally imposed
A squad room experiment shows that subliminal timing of light and vibration can shift decision-making by eight percentage points without subjects noticing
The leaked issue tracker reveals a philosophical debate: if you cause the outcomes you predict, you're not measuring prediction anymore—you're measuring control
The most insidious aspect is the bookkeeping—systems designed so that nothing illegal appears in any log file, with phone records, WiFi, and building systems all telling a clean story
Safety gates in the code (don't intervene if arousal is high, don't trigger if subject mentions self-harm) are mostly commented out
money_quotes: People call it 'mind control.' That's the wrong phrase. You hear that and you start hunting for sci-fi. What you should be hunting for is timing.
That's the thing about timing: when it works, it feels like you were going to do it anyway.

Mind control isn't the right term. It makes people look for sci-fi. The right term is 'nudge,' the one the elevator manuals use. You put your thumb on the timing. You don't push the person; you lean on the moment.
You can make a person look like a prediction if you take away all the moments where they would have surprised you.
The real trick isn't the tile or the lights. It's the bookkeeping. You arrange your systems so that there's nothing to subpoena.
If you want to find the devil, you don't go looking for horns. You go looking for clock edges.
One person said that was the point. Another said if your only wins are the ones you can cause you aren't measuring prediction anymore, you're measuring control.
posts.key_insight: The most dangerous form of control isn't force or even persuasion—it's precise timing that makes manipulation feel like free will, hidden behind systems designed to leave no evidence.

posts.summary: In this chapter of a serialized fiction story, the narrator discovers their mother is missing and enlists their tech-savvy friend Dave to help investigate. They find that someone from a VPN in Atlanta has been changing passwords on family accounts. Using a commercial dashcam data marketplace, they track the mother's car movements through Manhattan and Brooklyn. Despite finding a reassuring note from the mother claiming she went to the Hamptons, the narrator remains suspicious about the circumstances and the mysterious 'skinner666' who accessed her accounts.
key_points: The story explores a near-future surveillance economy where anyone can pay to search millions of dashcams and CCTV feeds for license plates or other queries
Dave's hatred of police stems from childhood memories of attending BLM protests with his mother and later harassment by child protective services after a friend's suicide
The narrator observes that ubiquitous surveillance didn't end crime because 'ending crime was always more a question of will than a question of ability'
The dashcam marketplace model only uploads video when purchased, meaning offline devices create gaps in the surveillance net
The chapter builds tension between a mundane explanation (mom left a note) and sinister possibilities (hacked accounts, forced note)
money_quotes: Don't let the being high all the time fool you, he was the smartest guy in our friend group.
Ending crime was always more a question of will than a question of ability.
And most people never made back the money they spent on the camera, the data wasn't all that useful and not that many people bought it.
It seemed like there was a whole Internet that I didn't know about.
One government mooching pig was the same as the next, and none of them were on his side.
You're the true crime guy. I'm just the tech guy.
Was this just me being paranoid, or is that the exact note my mother would leave if she didn't want me to worry and that's not at all what happened?
posts.key_insight: In a world of ubiquitous surveillance, the infrastructure exists to track anyone, but it's commodified, fragmented, and ultimately still leaves gaps that enable both privacy and crime.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that AI coding assistants are fundamentally just compilers that take English as input rather than a traditional programming language. He contends that English is actually a poor choice for specifying programs due to its imprecision, non-determinism, and non-local effects. The perceived value of AI coding tools is really an indictment of how bad existing programming languages, compilers, and libraries are. He cites a study showing AI makes developers feel 20% more productive while actually making them 19% slower, and criticizes the billions wasted on 'vibe coding' companies just as billions were wasted on flawed self-driving approaches.
key_points: AI coding assistants are best understood as compilers that take English prompts as input and output code
English is a poor programming language: imprecise, non-deterministic, and changes have non-local effects on output
The success of LLMs for coding reflects how bad existing tooling, languages, and codebases are, not how good AI is
AI will replace programming jobs the same way compilers and spreadsheets replaced jobs—as tools, not magic
A study shows AI makes developers feel 20% more productive but actually makes them 19% slower
money_quotes: Most people do not care to find the truth, they care about what pumps their bags.
Do you believe a compiler can code? If so, then go right on believing that AI can code.
The best model of a programming AI is a compiler.
tl;dr, you think AI coding is good because compilers, languages, and libraries are bad.
That anyone uses LLMs to code is a testament to just how bad tooling and languages are.
That LLMs can replace developers at companies is a testament to how bad that company's codebase and hiring bar is.
Is your way of thinking so fucking broken that you can't believe anyone cares more about the actual truth than make believe dollars?
Or we could, you know, do the hard work and build better programming languages, compilers, and libraries. But that can't be hyped up for billions.
posts.key_insight: AI coding tools are just compilers with English as the input language, and their perceived value says more about how bad our existing programming tools are than how good AI is.

posts.summary: Hotz delivers a scathing attack on people who entered the technology industry purely for money and status rather than genuine passion for building things. He accuses these 'entryists' of corrupting tech culture and calls for radical action: making everything free to drive them out. The post argues that the profit motive has attracted the wrong people to technology. His proposed solution is to eliminate the financial incentive entirely, leaving only those who truly care about the work.
key_points: Technology has been invaded by people motivated solely by money and status, not genuine interest
These 'entryists' are harmful to tech culture and need to be expelled
They won't leave voluntarily because they're still profiting
The solution is to make technology free, removing the financial incentive that attracted them
money_quotes: You heard there was money in tech. You heard there was status in tech. You showed up.
You never cared about technology. You cared about enriching yourself.
You are an entryist piece of shit. And it's time for you to leave.
Give it all away to everyone for free. Then you'll have no reason to be here.
posts.key_insight: The only way to purify technology culture is to eliminate profit entirely, because mercenaries only leave when there's nothing left to take.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that technology has been quietly captured by power structures in ways most people don't even remember or recognize. Drawing on the dystopian novel 'Manna,' he poses the fundamental question of whether machines will serve humanity or enslave it. He contends that centralized technology inevitably leads to a zero-sum power struggle that no one truly wins—even the final 'winner' ends up with worthless dominance. The post is a direct plea to technologists to stop building centralized systems and instead focus on decentralized alternatives before it's too late.
key_points: Tech culture has been infiltrated and transformed so gradually that people don't remember it was ever different
Centralized technology leads inevitably to slavery and endless power struggles with no real winners
The concentration of all power/money in one person makes that power meaningless
Technologists bear personal responsibility for the systems they build and where those systems lead society
The timeline for these consequences is not distant future but roughly 20 years away
money_quotes: Tech got inflitrated in such a way that you forgot that it was ever different.
Will the machines work for us or will we work for the machines?
People will not give up their desire for power over other people.
If technology is centralized, you will be a slave.
You will not be one of the 6 winners.
If one person ends up with all the money, the money is worthless.
Your vacations aren't worth it. Your house isn't worth it. Eating fancy food isn't worth it.
If everyone acted like you, where is the world going?
posts.key_insight: Centralized technology is a trap that leads to universal enslavement, and technologists must personally reckon with their role in building it before the 20-year countdown runs out.

posts.summary: Hotz delivers a scathing critique of people who chase external markers of success—high compensation, titles, prestigious credentials—rather than developing actual skills. He mimics the voice of someone obsessed with status symbols before dismissing them as losers who wasted their lives pursuing things 'made up by other men.' The post targets tech workers who optimized for career advancement metrics rather than genuine capability, and who now find themselves laid off despite their prestigious backgrounds.
key_points: External status markers (TC, titles, credentials, reports) are hollow achievements
Prestigious schools and compensation packages don't equal real value or importance
Chasing status instead of skill leads to vulnerability when layoffs come
The tech industry rewards people for playing status games rather than building real capabilities
True worth comes from skill, not from symbols created by others
money_quotes: I have big package. I am important.
You can see I am important? You can see I have big package?
you got laid off from an ad company after spending half your life chasing stuff made up by other men
you sought status instead of skill
you are a loser. get the fuck out
posts.key_insight: Optimizing for status symbols and career metrics instead of genuine skill leaves you with nothing when the music stops.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that robotics represents humanity's best chance to escape cloud computing's centralizing, freedom-eroding grip. Unlike web apps where economies of scale favor centralization, robotics has inherent bandwidth and latency requirements that make local compute necessary. He sees this as 'cloud-averse' technology that will naturally resist enshittification. The post connects comma.ai's mission to build 'one perfect box' to a broader vision of individual sovereignty through decentralized robotics, predicting that open source alternatives and on-device learning will keep the robotics ecosystem free from corporate control.
key_points: Technologies can be classified as cloud-favoring (Gmail), cloud-neutral (ChatGPT), or cloud-averse (robotics) based on their bandwidth/latency requirements
Robotics inherently resists cloud centralization because bandwidth and latency requirements are too high for remote compute
Open source alternatives provide 'checks and balances against enshittification' in cloud-averse domains
The future of robotics software is on-device learning with commodity foundation models, not cloud dependence
Physical problems like farming are non-adversarial, making robotics where 'real value in AI comes from'
money_quotes: The cloud is a highway to serfdom.
If the money and users went away, would you leave technology? If you were building this stuff for free for you and your 5 friends, would you keep at it?
Perelman proved the Poincaré conjecture for him and his boys; no businessfuckers showed up and put ads in the proof.
At comma, our goal is to build one perfect box that drives a car better than a human.
Technology itself is upstream of both politics and culture.
The companies will all try to shoehorn cloud into it, but like forcing you to create a cloud account for your microwave, people will see it as stupid.
The way the waves of robotics will break looks bright for individual sovereignty.
We will abolish scarcity; there will be nothing for them to steal they can't have for free.
posts.key_insight: Robotics' inherent need for local compute creates a technological path toward individual sovereignty that politics and culture alone cannot achieve.

posts.summary: George Hotz argues that America's economic decline stems from an unsustainable ratio of unproductive to productive people, making democratic reform impossible. After visiting Chinese factories and witnessing their straightforward, competitive manufacturing approach, he contrasts this with America's rent-seeking systems and bureaucratic bloat. With 28% of voters on Social Security alone—plus countless others in 'made up fake systems'—he believes America has been '51% attacked' by people voting themselves benefits. He suggests only a 'good dictator' could reverse course, pointing to productive immigrants like Elon Musk and Jensen Huang as America's remaining competitive hope against China.
key_points: Chinese factories operate with transparent confidence—they'll tell you exactly what they're doing because they know you can't compete anyway
America has crossed the threshold where more than half the population is economically 'unproductive,' making democratic reform structurally impossible
Social Security recipients alone represent 28% of voters, not counting other groups in artificially complex systems that 'should go away'
The only path forward may be benevolent authoritarianism, though the risk of South American-style dictatorship looms
Productive capacity determines geopolitical power—100 CNC machines beat 100 lawyers in any real conflict
money_quotes: I'm gonna tell you what I'm doing, and you can try to compete, but I'll still crush you.
Jail for the cronies and rent-seekers, wireheading city for the homeless, and no more medicare or social security.
There's everyone who is working in made up fake systems where both sides ratchet up complexity when really the whole thing should go away.
I think the most people are unproductive ship has sailed. America got 51% attacked.
Can we get a good dictator and not a South America style one?
Would you bet on 100 CNC machines or 100 lawyers?
posts.key_insight: When the unproductive majority can vote themselves benefits from the productive minority, democracy becomes a mechanism for economic decline rather than reform.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that gambling represents a fundamental societal sickness where people have given up believing that effort leads to improvement. He connects this to UBI, volatile government policies, and crypto speculation—all of which undermine the link between work and reward. The post announces his move to Hong Kong, which he frames as escaping a 'fake' US economy built on value cannibalization rather than creation. He predicts America faces a 'century of humiliation' before rebuilding, with recovery only coming when 'money is real again.'
key_points: Human motivation depends on believing effort will improve one's life—policies like UBI that sever this connection create demotivation
Gambling (including crypto and investments) represents accepting that luck, not effort, determines outcomes
Gambling is zero-sum and creates no value, meaning a society of gamblers inevitably loses collectively
The US economy is fundamentally 'fake'—built on cannibalization rather than value creation—and will decline over 20 years
America will recover eventually, but only after a period of humiliation and when 'money is real again'
money_quotes: Will my life improve if I put in effort.
This is why things like UBI are a terrible idea. If you are at the baseline and don't see a walkable path to rise above it, you won't.
Gambling is the ultimate expression of this acceptance.
The fundamental problem with gambling is that it creates no value. It's completely zero sum, and that means in the long run in a society of gamblers everyone loses.
If you made $100k in a casino on a hot run in one night, how do you motivate yourself to put in a year's worth of effort to earn that?
I think people still have no idea how fake the US economy is, and how bad it will get over the next 20 years.
If nobody is creating the value and everyone is trying to cannibalize, eventually there is nothing left.
You'll know things are turning around when the money is real again.
posts.key_insight: A society where people believe luck matters more than effort—expressed through gambling, speculation, and policy volatility—will inevitably collapse as value creation gives way to zero-sum cannibalization.

posts.summary: Hotz documents his attempt to replace his MacBook with a Linux laptop, specifically an HP ZBook with AMD's Strix Halo chip. While he's made progress on sleep power management (0.2W lid-closed), active use still draws 7-10W compared to MacBook's efficiency, giving only 7 hours of battery life. He identifies the CPU's chiplet design as the main culprit, drawing 4W at idle versus Apple's 1W. He calls on AMD and HP to release better documentation and argues that with proper optimization, a competitive Linux laptop is achievable.
key_points: Successfully switched from iPhone to Samsung Z Fold, but laptops are harder because no hardware matches MacBook quality
Asahi Linux isn't viable long-term due to Apple's custom MCUs and missing features like external USB-C monitor support
AMD Strix Halo's chiplet architecture is power-hungry, drawing 4W at idle versus Apple M3 Max's 1W
LLMs are making reverse engineering fun again by helping decode embedded controller bytes and power management
Hardware vendors should release schematics or at least block diagrams like Framework does
money_quotes: Sadly, there's no better laptop hardware than a MacBook.
It fixed the problem I always had with Arch, which is how customizable it is.
LLMs are super helpful at reverse engineering – it's almost becoming fun again.
That's only 7 hours of life, which is 30% of a MacBook, and still not really acceptable.
You know MoP is a good idea because Intel is discontinuing it while Apple keeps doing it.
Don't turn on the stupid keyboard backlight that draws 2 watts!
I bet there's something stupid or poorly designed, HP should release a schematic of the laptop.
posts.key_insight: Despite AMD's Strix Halo being the only chip rivaling Apple silicon in performance, its chiplet design and lack of vendor transparency on power management make Linux laptops still far behind MacBooks in battery efficiency.

posts.summary: George Hotz expresses frustration with the current state of laptops, arguing that Apple's quality is declining while Linux alternatives like Asahi fail due to Apple's closed hardware. He critiques Framework's focus on upgradability as a misguided tradeoff that sacrifices build quality and polish. He proposes a hypothetical 'hackbook' - a single-configuration, no-compromise Linux laptop built on AMD's Strix Halo with obsessive attention to polish and simple yearly iteration. The post concludes with him hoping someone else will build this so he doesn't have to, acknowledging he's bikeshedding rather than committing to the project.
key_points: Apple's increasing use of closed-source microcontrollers makes Linux ports like Asahi fundamentally limited - they'll never achieve full polish
Framework's modular philosophy trades away weight, rigidity, and polish for upgradability that power users don't actually need
Consumer electronics naming should be dead simple: good product, simple name, increment the number each year
The best developers are what make Apple's ecosystem work - if they leave, Apple loses its secret advantage
Engineering is about tradeoffs, and the right tradeoff for a developer laptop is zero customization in exchange for maximum polish
money_quotes: My dream laptop is simple, a MacBook with Linux, supported by a company that is user aligned.
Like jailbreaking, it may start off strong when people are excited, but support for the next generation and that last bit of polish won't ever get there.
I don't care one bit about upgradability or customizability. After a year or two, I'm happy to throw it out and buy a new one.
Engineering is all about trade-offs, and the trade-off I'd prefer is 0 upgradability or customizability in exchange for less weight and more polish.
Consumer electronics naming is very simple. Make a good product with a simple name.

Apple will not understand what they had until they lose it, the only reason anything works on Mac at all is because there's 100,000 amazing developers who use these machines every day; they put some work into making their house nice.
For some reason people say I get distracted, but comma has been around for ten years following this playbook.
Can somebody please build a good MacBook replacement and make it a Schelling point everyone will switch to so I don't have to think about this anymore?
posts.key_insight: The laptop market is failing power users because companies optimize for the wrong tradeoffs - modularity over polish, product line complexity over simplicity - leaving a gap for a user-aligned company to build one excellent, no-compromise Linux machine.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that the breakthrough in AI computer use wasn't desktop emulation with GUI interaction, but rather command-line interfaces like Claude Code. He's impressed by Opus 4.5's ability to reverse engineer apps and write Python scripts, seeing it as a way to bypass ads and dark patterns. While acknowledging the model still lacks 'taste' as a programmer, he notes the agentic loop allows it to iterate rapidly. He pushes back against claims he's late to adopt these tools, arguing Opus 4.5 is genuinely the first model capable of real computer use.
key_points: The real computer use breakthrough was command-line agents, not GUI/desktop emulation
Opus 4.5 dramatically outperforms open models (GLM, Qwen3, gpt-oss) and even GPT 5.2 in agentic loops
AI can reverse engineer apps and bypass dark patterns, acting as an 'elite computer hacker'
The model lacks taste as a programmer (like it lacks taste as a rapper), but persistence compensates
Reinforcement learning on long contexts appears to be the key training approach that makes this work
money_quotes: Turns out the idea wasn't a desktop emulator with a keyboard and mouse, it was just a command line.
Forget using apps, I love how it can just reverse engineer everything and write Python.
Ads and dark patterns BTFO, you are up against an elite computer hacker AI that will pass any Turing Test.
I still think it's a bad programmer, largely for the same reason it's a bad rapper. It lacks taste, and it's unclear how to teach it this.
The local agentic loop allows it to just keep trying, it's fast and persistent.
Opus 4.5 is the first model I feel that can use computers at all.
posts.key_insight: The path to useful AI computer agents wasn't through mimicking human GUI interaction, but through leveraging the command line where AI's ability to rapidly iterate and reverse engineer compensates for its lack of taste.

posts.summary: Hotz defends his prediction that Tesla is 8 years away from fully solving self-driving, while maintaining Tesla will solve it first. He dismisses critics as politically-minded stock shills rather than people with genuine technical understanding. He defines 'solved' as driving on par with skilled humans in every scenario, not merely being useful. He draws a parallel to AI coding—useful but not yet human-level—and embraces his role as someone whose nuanced takes anger partisans on all sides.
key_points: Tesla FSD is 8 years from being truly finished, meaning it drives as well as skilled humans in every scenario with zero mistakes
Being useful (like comma.ai already is) is different from being finished—the bar is repeatedly betting your life on cross-country drives
AI coding assistants are better than beginners but not yet better than skilled humans, similar to how minimax beats novices at chess
Critics mistake anecdotal success (1,000 miles of self-driving) for technical understanding
Nuanced opinions that don't fit tribal narratives anger people on all sides
money_quotes: At least FSD is real AI that you can evalulate the honest progress of, unlike remote control cars that break when the power goes out.
I'm constantly trying the latest stuff, I got a ride in a Cybertruck from JFK to downtown Manhattan two days ago (one intervention).
That you'd repeatedly bet your life on it driving across the country without making a single mistake.
AI's will eventually become better programmers than humans, they just aren't yet.
I like AI, I want it to be good, it will someday be good, and right now it's kind of good, but it's not that good yet.
But commenters seem to be dumb politically minded shills who need to sort every comment into with us or against us.
I'm against all of you and with the truth.
posts.key_insight: Nuanced technical assessments that don't fit neat tribal narratives—acknowledging something is promising while noting its current limitations—will anger partisans who demand you pick a side.

posts.summary: George Hotz reflects on five years of building tinygrad, a minimal deep learning framework that now stands at 18,935 lines of code with a team of 6 people. He argues that the path to competing with NVIDIA is through software sovereignty, not hardware—pointing out that AMD, Amazon, Tesla, and Groq have all made good chips but failed at training because they lack the software stack. Tinygrad is working toward zero dependencies and LLVM removal to drive AMD GPUs with pure Python. Hotz critiques mainstream software development as 98% workarounds for other code, advocating for Elon's principle that 'the best part is no part.' The tiny corp operates as a 'deconstructed company' with public Discord, GitHub, AMD contracts negotiated on Twitter, and a mission to commoditize the petaflop.
key_points: Software sovereignty, not chip design, is the hard part—Google and NVIDIA dominate training because of software, not silicon
Tinygrad aims for zero dependencies (pure Python only) and will be about 20,000 lines when complete
98% of software lines are workarounds for issues in other parts of the codebase, a dysfunction that also plagues organizations
The tiny corp operates as a 'deconstructed company' with radical transparency—public Discord, GitHub, and contracts negotiated on Twitter
The company funds itself through $2M/year in computer sales and an AMD contract for MI350X MLPerf benchmarks
money_quotes: Only a fool begins by taping out a chip; it's expensive and not the hard part.
Once you have a fully sovereign software stack capable of training SOTA models, the chip is so easy.
I think this is so bad that 98% of lines of software are basically this in some way shape or form.
Make the requirements less dumb. The best part is no part.
Most requirements in software exist to maintain compatibility with other abstractions.
Each piece of code in that stack isn't focused on the goal. It's focused on the other pieces of code.
Like how fancy chefs will deconstruct dishes, the tiny corp is a deconstructed company.
Our mission is to commoditize the petaflop.
posts.key_insight: The path to competing with NVIDIA isn't building better chips—it's building a fully sovereign, minimal software stack, because software is the actual moat.

posts.summary: George Hotz criticizes Anthropic for blocking opencode from the Claude Code API, viewing it as hostile to users and a strategic mistake. He argues that rather than forcing users back to Claude Code, this action will drive them to competing model providers. The post warns that this sets a precedent where Anthropic may cut off any users who don't use their products in approved ways.
key_points: Anthropic blocked opencode from accessing the Claude Code API for a second time
User-hostile actions drive customers to competitors rather than back to official products
Platform lock-in attempts signal that any non-sanctioned usage patterns may be cut off
The timing is particularly poor given Opus 4.5's success and growing user adoption
Easy workarounds exist now, but harder enforcement could follow
money_quotes: But this level of user hostility raises the question, is this really a company you want to rely on in your workflow?
First they came for opencode And I did not speak out Because I did not use opencode
You will not convert people back to Claude Code, you will convert people to other model providers.
These actions make it clear that opencode is only the start.
If you aren't using Claude Code how they want you to, they will cut you off.
posts.key_insight: Restricting how users can access your API doesn't create loyalty—it creates motivation to find alternatives.

posts.summary: Hotz warns that talented tech workers are unwittingly building a 'neofeudal' future where AI and automation will marginalize all labor, making their accumulated wealth worthless. He argues that in a world where capital is the only force, advanced AI systems will inevitably separate workers from whatever they've saved through advertising, scams, or government lobbying. Unlike historical feudalism where peasants had value as labor, the machine-driven future offers no such protection. His solution: refuse to participate in building these systems.
key_points: Money accumulated now won't protect you when AI can outmaneuver humans at every turn—GPT$$$ will find ways to extract whatever you have
Historical feudalism needed peasants for labor; neofeudalism won't, eliminating any leverage workers currently have
The neofeudal world won't operate on capitalist principles, so capitalist strategies like accumulating assets won't work
Working at large tech companies actively accelerates the system that will eventually consume the workers building it
The only solution is collective non-participation—individual escape is impossible
money_quotes: In the future, when labor is fully marginalized and capital is the only force, you will not be able to afford GPT$$$ (it's $1B per month), only the billionaires will.
GPT$$$ is surely smart enough to separate you from whatever you have, be that with targeting advertising, a scam you fall for, or lobbying your government to take it from you.
A pile of money will buy you nothing in the neofeudal world.
When the grain is produced by machines, the peasants are cut out of the loop.
They will find a way to make whatever you have worthless.
If you work at a large company... you are actively bringing about the system that will kill you.
We will either all be in the underclass together or not.
posts.key_insight: Tech workers building AI systems are sawing off the branch they're sitting on—no amount of personal wealth accumulation will protect them from the labor-marginalizing future they're creating.

posts.summary: Hotz defends his track record of avoiding big tech capture and explains his philosophy of building companies that resist centralized control. He argues that the future depends on who truly 'owns' the hardware and AI training infrastructure, not in a legal sense but in the hacker sense of having root access. Through comma.ai and tiny corp, he's pursuing a strategy of commoditizing compute and ensuring open source prevents rent-seeking pivots. He challenges readers to stop participating in systems that concentrate power, arguing that buying personal safety is both impossible and contemptible.
key_points: True ownership means having root access and control, not legal title - this will determine what the future looks like
NVIDIA's dominance comes from software lock-in, not hardware superiority, which is why commoditizing the petaflop matters
MIT licensing open source software is a defensive mechanism that prevents future pivots to rent-seeking even if company control is lost
Individual defection (selling out for personal safety) is a losing strategy that dooms everyone collectively
Sustainable profitability through simple business models (selling boxes for more than they cost) beats hypergrowth ponzi schemes
money_quotes: I think who owns the robots is going to be a key aspect of what the future looks like. And I don't mean 'owns' from a legalist perspective, I mean 'owns' as in the hacker meaning, like 'owning' the box. Who has root?
NVIDIA's value comes from their software, not their hardware, what else explains the huge gap between NVIDIA and AMD's market cap?
If you think you can somehow just buy safety for yourself, you are both wrong and pathetic.
The best hope any of us have is to maximize the number of things that survive.
If you and everyone else sell to bankers in hopes of buying a personal ticket out, we are all dead.
I'm not playing defect, and I shame you for doing so.
To everyone working on ads, surveillance, gambling, secret research, enshittification, cloud lock-in, what are you doing with your life?
It doesn't require everyone to stop, just enough people. And it starts with you.
posts.key_insight: The future will be determined by who has true control over AI hardware and training infrastructure, making commoditization and open source critical strategies for preventing dystopian concentration of power.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that robotaxis will follow the same consolidation playbook as other Silicon Valley ventures: initial VC-funded proliferation of dozens of competing companies, followed by inevitable consolidation down to 2-3 monopolistic players who will then raise prices using algorithmic price discrimination. Unlike Uber and Lyft, robotaxis eliminate the 'analog hole' where riders can negotiate directly with drivers, leaving personal car ownership as the only competitive check on pricing. He predicts the industry will then systematically eliminate car ownership through insurance manipulation, ultimately giving a few corporations control over all personal transportation. He contrasts this with China, where state power keeps corporations in check, and warns that losing car ownership means losing autonomy over where and when you can travel.
key_points: Robotaxis will follow the scooter company playbook: VC-funded proliferation of ~26 identical companies, massive unprofitability, then consolidation to 2-3 dominant players who coordinate pricing
Unlike ride-sharing, robotaxis eliminate the 'analog hole' — you can't negotiate with a robot, so the only remaining price check is personal car ownership
Car ownership won't be made illegal but will be priced out through insurance companies refusing to insure human drivers, leaving only a $75k self-insurance bond as an option
The loss of car ownership represents a broader pattern of personal property becoming corporate-controlled services with changing terms and restrictions on when and where you can travel
China's model, where corporations self-regulate under implicit state pressure, may produce better outcomes for consumers than America's consolidation-to-monopoly trajectory
money_quotes: Of course they won't, but they didn't make general computation illegal either. And yet, who has root on the computer you are reading this on?
Everyone should know there's 25 other identical companies, they will have secrecy vibes trying to claim they figured out some key detail the others didn't.

Everyone tacitly agrees that the correct price for a ride has nothing to do with the cost of providing that ride, just simply the algorithmically calculated maximum amount the purchaser is willing to pay.
With robotaxis, you can't do this. There's 0 fear that a robotaxi will defect from your network.
They will just raise the price through insurance. Who is going to insure a human driver?
This is simply what's going to happen. The effective end of car ownership, I don't see anything in America that will stop it.
It's not just about cars, it's about something that was yours becoming just another service with a license that has changing terms from week to week.
Wait where are you going? Oh it's 2 AM and that's an area with prostitution we aren't going to service rides to that area. Surely you don't need freedom. You trust the corporation.
posts.key_insight: Robotaxis will eliminate the analog hole that keeps ride-sharing prices in check, and the resulting monopolies will systematically destroy personal car ownership through insurance manipulation, converting transportation from a personal right into a corporate-controlled service.

posts.summary: Hotz lays out his vision of personally owning a zettaflop computer — a machine with 1e21 FLOPS, equivalent to one million Claudes or 50,000 human workers. He frames this as the natural progression from comma.ai's current exaflop-scale compute, arguing the main bottlenecks are power and chip cost rather than fundamental physics. He sketches a concrete plan involving 250 acres of solar panels, 100,000 chips at $100 each, and a total cost around $30M. The post mixes genuine technical calculation with an almost spiritual desire to command vast computational power, quoting Vernor Vinge's vision of overwhelming sensory bandwidth.
key_points: A zettaflop (1e21 FLOPS) would enable the equivalent of a human year of cognitive work in 10 minutes, like having 50,000 aligned workers
The biggest bottleneck is power, not compute architecture — current B200s deliver 10 TFLOPS/W, but 10x improvement would bring requirements to a manageable 10 MW
Hotz estimates the total cost at roughly $30M: $10M for chips, $10M for solar, $10M for land and construction
tinygrad is positioned as the missing software layer needed to command exaflop-scale compute as a unified system
The gap between prediction and action is a recurring theme — knowing the future of compute doesn't automatically prepare you for it
money_quotes: As any self driving car maker knows, predicting doesn't mean you can act.
A gigawatt of power, a million GPUs, 1000 exaflops, a zettaflop.
I want to feel it. I want to command that kind of power.
I already have one of these, I just don't have the software finished yet to command it all together.

One million Claudes. To be able to search every book in history, solve math problems, write novels, read every comment, watch every reel, iterate over and over on a piece of code until it's perfect – spend a human year in 10 minutes.
50,000 people working for you, all aligned with you, all answering as one.
The biggest bottleneck is power.
I'll own this before I die.
posts.key_insight: Personal ownership of a zettaflop — a million-Claude machine — is achievable within a lifetime at roughly $30M, with power generation being the primary engineering challenge rather than compute itself.

posts.summary: Hotz critiques Dario Amodei's vision of AI development as dangerously centralized and top-down, arguing that essays like 'Machines of Loving Grace' assume a narrow worldview where a few 'adults' control AI to fix predetermined problems. He contends that the EA movement shares this flaw of treating desired outcomes as self-evident. Instead, he advocates for radically decentralized AI development—a million independent AIs raised in diverse contexts rather than one datacenter—arguing that the only existential risk from AI is centralized control, not AI itself. He dismisses UBI as serfdom and calls for open source as the real path to equality.
key_points: Amodei's AI vision assumes top-down control as a given, which Hotz sees as the actual danger rather than the safeguard
EA's critical flaw is treating desired outcomes as so obvious they don't need discussion, creating a with-us-or-against-us dynamic
The only way AI ends badly on a cosmic scale is if a singular entity or ideologically homogeneous group holds overwhelming power
Decentralized AI development across millions of independent actors is preferable even if messy and violent, because diversity of goals and values is what matters for long-term survival
UBI is reframed as serfdom while open source is positioned as the real mechanism for reducing inequality
money_quotes: It assumes the perspective of a top-down ruler, that someone can and will get to control AI. This is taken as a given.
They assume that the desired outcome is so obvious that it's not worth discussing, it's only worth discussing how to achieve it.
Instead, imagine the births of geniuses to a million mothers across the world.

The beautiful thing about those million is that some will be terrorists, some religious fanatics, some pornographers, some criminals, some plant lovers, etc… They will not be controlled and birthed by a singular homogenous entity.
lowering inequality doesn't look like UBI, it looks like open source. UBI is serfdom.
There's only one way AI ends badly on a cosmic scale, and that's if a singular entity has overwhelming power.
It doesn't matter if they do, the boot is still stamping on the human face – forever.
Stop centralizing technology. Work to decentralize it.
posts.key_insight: The real existential risk from AI isn't the technology itself but the centralization of its control, and the solution is radical decentralization even at the cost of messy, violent diversity.

posts.summary: Hotz revisits his 2019 prediction about the singularity arriving soon and bringing horrific consequences if power-seeking motivations persist. He argues that society has lost its way, building technology that serves exploitation rather than genuine human benefit, contrasting Craigslist's simplicity with Shein's consumerism. He links to 'schizoposting' essays by Alaric that frame the core problem as cultural rather than technological, comparing them to Mark Fisher's 'Capitalist Realism.' The post is a short, frustrated call for collective action and cultural revolution against systems that concentrate power while hollowing out meaning.
key_points: Hotz claims vindication for his 2019 prediction that the singularity would arrive soon and be dangerous if power motivations remained unchanged
The real problem isn't AI itself but the culture and power structures directing its development — technology could serve people but instead serves exploitation
He contrasts Craigslist (technology as public utility) with Shein (technology as extractive consumerism) as two poles of what tech can be
He endorses Alaric's 'schizoposting' essays as a modern successor to Mark Fisher's Capitalist Realism, framing the crisis as cultural stagnation and manufactured conflict
He calls for collective defection and revolution, asking how to form a tribe against the current trajectory
money_quotes: How is everyone enjoying their singularity?
Why are we letting the minds that invented fastpass run things?
We live in a society. It seems a lot of people have forgotten this.
So much stuff that's being built just shouldn't be built.
You know technology could be good, right?
Is everyone individually too weak to defect? Sounds like we need a revolution.
It makes clear the problem isn't AI, it's culture.
posts.key_insight: The danger of the singularity isn't artificial intelligence itself but the cultural and power structures directing it — technology built to concentrate power rather than serve people will produce dystopia regardless of how advanced it becomes.

posts.summary: George Hotz outlines tiny corp's long-term product vision: a local training box that learns and adapts its weights based on individual user interactions, unlike current LLMs that serve identical frozen models to everyone. He argues that the current trajectory of AI leads to a collapse in cognitive diversity, where billions of people interact with the same handful of homogeneous minds differentiated only by shallow prompt customization. Hotz contends that cloud-based AI will win by default unless local models can offer genuine per-user learning through weight updates, not just in-context learning. He positions the tinybox hardware, tinygrad infrastructure, and OpenClaw/opencode frontends as the foundation for this vision. The post warns that people who rely on homogeneous cloud AI will become superfluous middlemen, ultimately cut out of the economic loop. While acknowledging this vision is years away, he frames it as building something fundamentally different: an AI that lives in your house and learns your values.
key_points: Current LLMs create a dangerous monoculture where everyone shares the same weights, desires, and biases — a collapse in cognitive diversity
Local models can only beat cloud if they offer genuine per-user weight updates (learning), not just prompt customization which Hotz dismisses as 'costuming'
The dream of AI doing your job while you get paid is economically impossible — if you're superfluous, you'll be cut out as a middleman
Tiny corp's product vision is a tinybox that updates weights based on user interactions, combining their hardware, tinygrad infrastructure, and OpenClaw/opencode frontends
Enshittification won't drive local adoption — either open models are too bad to matter or good enough that cloud competition prevents abuse
money_quotes: Every month, we see these LLMs become more and more human. However, there's a major difference. They do not learn.
Everyone has the same Claude/Codex/Kimi, with the same weights, the same desires, and the same biases. If current trends continue, the collapse in diversity will be staggering.
I think there is a world market for maybe five people.
The open question is if everything that's unique about you can fit in a 10 kB CLAUDE.md.
This isn't learning, it's costuming. Everyone who has used these things knows how little of an impact prompting makes compared to the model.
If you choose the homogenous mind, you are superfluous and will be cut out.
The dream is an AI product that will do your job for you while you continue to get paid. But this cannot exist, that's way too much of a fee to pay to the middleman.
Not API keyed SaaS clones. Something that lives in your house and learns your values. Your child.
posts.key_insight: The future of AI must involve local models that genuinely learn and update their weights per user, because without real personalization beyond shallow prompting, humans become interchangeable middlemen to homogeneous cloud minds.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that the housing affordability crisis is fundamentally unsolvable because existing homeowners—many of whom are leveraged through mortgages—cannot afford for prices to drop. He frames the situation as a generational wealth transfer problem: boomers were promised that homes are appreciating assets, and the entire system is now structured to maintain that fiction. He dismisses common explanations like zoning and building costs as missing the real issue, which is that current owners need to offload their investment onto new buyers.
key_points: Falling housing prices would devastate existing homeowners, especially those with mortgages who would go underwater
The system is locked into ensuring housing prices never decline because an entire generation's wealth depends on it
Common policy explanations for the housing crisis (zoning, building costs, environmental reviews) are distractions from the core problem
The housing market functions as a generational bag-holding scheme where current owners need new buyers to absorb inflated prices
money_quotes: Many people in America are complaining about the cost of housing. But do they understand the damage it will do if it prices go down?
It's simply out of the question for housing prices to go down.
If you want to buy a house to live in, sorry.
The boomers were told houses are appreciating assets, and now we must bend reality to make that true.
Until you solve this problem, you will never solve the housing affordability crisis.
It has nothing to do with zoning, building costs, or environmental reviews. It has to do with people holding bags they need to dump on you.
posts.key_insight: The housing affordability crisis is structurally unsolvable because the entire system is designed to protect existing homeowners' investments, making affordability for new buyers fundamentally incompatible with the promises made to current owners.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that AI will only ruin art that was already bad — formulaic, derivative content like Marvel movies and generic pop music. He distinguishes between 'slop' that most people consume passively and genuine art that pushes cultural boundaries. He predicts 95% of people will end up 'wireheaded' on AI-generated content loops, while real art will remain human-driven. AI tools will assist production but can't replace the cultural embeddedness and boundary-pushing that defines true art. The post concludes that AI making bad art cheap is actually a good thing, since it exposes and replaces what was never real art to begin with.
key_points: AI-generated content is indistinguishable from already-formulaic human content like Marvel movies and generic pop music, which were effectively algorithmic already
Real art is defined by cultural embeddedness, rarity, and pushing boundaries — qualities that current AI fundamentally cannot produce
Most people prefer comfortable loops of derivative content ('slop') and will happily consume AI-generated versions, effectively becoming 'wireheaded'
AI will serve as a production tool for real artists (as computers already do) but the creative locus of control for good art will remain human
The real losers from AI art are not artists but the 'algorithmically driven sell outs' who produced focus-grouped content for money
money_quotes: For many people, all they want is slop.
I see a world where 95% of people end up basically wireheaded.
Art is defined as what pushes the boundaries of civilization.
As long as civilization is human, the loci of control of good art will remain human.
Bad art will be cheap to make.
That was never made by real artists anyway, just algorithmically driven sell outs.
Art is defined by what is expensive. What is rare. What is expectation breaking. What is embedded in a complex and thriving culture. Not slop produced by a parrot like Marvel movies.
posts.key_insight: AI doesn't threaten real art — it only automates the formulaic content that was never genuine art in the first place, which should be celebrated rather than mourned.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that AI is dismantling the trillion-dollar rent-seeking economy built on exploiting human time limitations and friction. He traces this from Google Duplex's suppressed demo to the current landscape where Chinese open-source models are democratizing human-level AI. He critiques Anthropic's distillation blog post as the 'last gasps' of companies trying to maintain artificial moats, and argues the AI supply chain is commoditizing at every layer except possibly models—where Chinese open-source efforts are closing the gap. He concludes that AI agents will eliminate the time asymmetry businesses exploit, making purposeful friction obsolete.
key_points: The US economy built trillions in enterprise value on exploiting human time limitations through friction—call centers, confusing pricing, procedural barriers—and AI eliminates that asymmetry
Human-level AI ending up accessible to individuals rather than locked behind proprietary platforms was not inevitable, but happened due to simple token-based APIs and Chinese state priorities

The AI supply chain has five tiers (electricity, chip manufacturing, chip design/software, models, applications), and most are commoditizing except models—where Chinese open-source is rapidly closing the gap
Anthropic's anti-distillation blog post represents a desperate attempt to preserve artificial moats, and the industry should refuse to 'coordinate' against open models
China's strategic interest in commoditizing AI models aligns with their strength in chip production and electricity, following the 'commoditize your complement' playbook
money_quotes: Enter AI, the great equalizer of time.
Cable companies and insurance rely on the fact that your time is more valuable than theirs. They can hire people in India at scale to waste your time.
Godspeed to anyone who was dumb enough to invest in a GPT wrapper company.
Nobody should 'coordinate' with them. They stand alone as a vanguard of the rent seeking apparatus that is long past its expiration date.
Because nobody wants the continuation of rent-seeking billionaires. The status quo is cooked. It's time to flip the table, not rearrange the seats.
Nobody should build an economy based on rent seeking and increasing friction. I pray the collapse will be swift and legible so that reconstruction (in the right way) can begin as soon as possible.
The era of purposefully frustrating humans is over.
When you call, e-mail, text, or show me an ad, you'll never know if it's me or my model seeing it.
posts.key_insight: AI destroys the time asymmetry that businesses exploit against consumers, and Chinese open-source models are ensuring this power ends up with individuals rather than creating a new rent-seeking layer.

posts.summary: Hotz argues that Universal Basic Income is fundamentally flawed because its proponents misunderstand the nature of money and economics. He contends that UBI experiments only work at small scale because recipients spend money in an economy where producers aren't also on UBI. At universal scale, he claims UBI would cause massive inflation and reduced production as workers quit, leaving everyone worse off. He frames UBI advocates as people who see themselves apart from society, not understanding that goods require labor to produce. His alternative prescription is simply making everything cheaper to produce, though he notes regulatory obstacles prevent this.
key_points: Small-scale UBI experiments are misleading because they don't capture the universal effects on production and prices
Universal implementation would simultaneously reduce labor supply (fewer goods produced) and increase demand (more money chasing goods), causing severe inflation
UBI proponents suffer from a 'stuff fairy' fallacy — treating the economy as a natural phenomenon rather than a system of mutual labor
The real solution is making production cheaper, but regulatory barriers (like memory chip production concerns) actively prevent this
Money is a map of economic relationships, not wealth itself — redistributing the map doesn't change the territory
money_quotes: Thinking that UBI will solve anything comes from a misunderstanding about money.
Want to buy eggs? Sorry, the egg people stopped making eggs, they are living free on UBI.
Belief in UBI comes from a fallacy that the economy is some natural thing.
The root of this stupidity is that people see themselves apart from society.
They don't know where stuff comes from, it might as well be the stuff fairy that puts it on the supermarket shelves and sets prices.
There already is UBI in the world for some people, it's called allowance. It's for children and high-end prostitutes.
What comes first, actually trying UBI or the end of democracy?
Like most things politicians invent, nobody considered the second order effects, and you will not be getting 2,000 eggs with your UBI.
posts.key_insight: UBI fails because money is a claim on other people's labor, and when everyone stops laboring, there's nothing left to claim — you can't redistribute production that no longer exists.

posts.summary: Hotz walks back the anxiety-inducing rhetoric common in AI hype culture, including his own previous provocative takes. He argues that social media is deliberately targeting people with fear about AI falling behind, and that this is toxic nonsense. AI is framed as a continuation of existing computational progress — search and optimization — not a magical paradigm shift. The real economic threat isn't AI itself but rent-seeking jobs being consolidated by larger players who use 'AI' as a convenient narrative. His core prescription is to stop playing zero-sum games and instead focus on creating net positive value for others.
key_points: AI hype on social media is deliberately engineered to trigger fear and anxiety, pushing people into irrational workflow panic
AI is not a qualitative leap — it's search and optimization, a continuation of the same exponential progress curve, with real limits knowable from CS fundamentals
Layoffs are driven by large players consolidating rent-seeking positions, not by AI replacing workers — 'AI' is just the PR cover story
Zero-sum games (rent-seeking, comparison traps) are the real trap; the exit is to create more value than you consume
Calibrated contentment — not infinite growth, not always needing more — is a viable and sufficient life strategy
money_quotes: Social media has been extremely toxic for the last couple months. It's targeting you with fear and anxiety.
AI is not a magical game changer, it's simply the continuation of the exponential of progress we have been on for a long time.
People see 'AI' and they attribute some sci-fi thing to it when it's just search and optimization. Always has been.
If you have a job where you create complexity for others, you will be found out.
They just say it's AI cause that makes the stock price go up.
Go create value for others and don't worry about the returns.
The world is not a Red Queen's race.
This post will get way less traction than the doom ones, but it's telling you the way out.
posts.key_insight: The AI anxiety loop is a manufactured social media phenomenon masking mundane economic consolidation by large players — the exit is creating genuine value rather than competing in zero-sum games.

posts.summary: Geohot sarcastically 'changes his mind' on UBI after receiving an email arguing that UBI would drive people to be self-sufficient. He follows the email's logic to its conclusion: inflation from UBI would drive people toward barter, then commodity money (gold), creating a parallel economy that outcompetes the fiat/UBI economy. He notes that DOGE has failed to actually shrink government spending, and that entitlement programs are politically untouchable. His real argument is that the only way to kill Social Security and Medicare is to let UBI-style money printing destroy the value of fiat currency entirely — making entitlement payouts worthless.
key_points: UBI would cause inflation, which would push people toward productive self-sufficiency (growing chickens) and eventually toward commodity-backed barter economies.
A gold-based parallel economy would outcompete the UBI dollar economy due to the absence of the subsidy drag.
Despite DOGE noise, the US federal budget actually increased 3.1% from 2024 to 2025 — government never shrinks itself.
60% of the federal budget is 'Mandatory' entitlement spending — politically immune to cuts.
The only way to eliminate Social Security is to keep it but inflate the currency until the payouts are worthless — 'the only way out is through.'
money_quotes: Unlike maybe something like free speech, you have no human right to food, because that would imply you have the right to steal from people who grow food.
Because there isn't this massive drain on the new economy subsidizing all these people who aren't working, the gold economy is way more efficient than the UBI dollar economy.
In hushed whispers among the productive, 'err, I don't really want dollars, you got gold?'
Despite all the noise made about DOGE and cutting, the budget from 2024 to 2025 went up 3.1%. What's an extra $210B among friends?
There's Discretionary (27%), which is everything a government should do, and there's Mandatory (60%) which is entitlement programs that give money to old people.
The only way to get rid of Social Security... Keep the entitlement programs, just have them pay out fake worthless money. The only way out is through.
Free money for corporations! Free money for illegal immigrants! Free money for the whole damn planet! Where does this free money come from? Print it!
posts.key_insight: UBI's real value isn't social welfare — it's that hyperinflation from printing money for everyone is the only politically viable path to making entitlement programs irrelevant.
